# Paper Update Package

## 1. Add a new “Current package frontier” subsection

**Placement:** after the existing UV / collision-blow-up / corrected-whitening discussion, preferably immediately after the staged UV-025 tagged source-block insertion and before any claims about UV-023/024/026 quotient invisibility.

```tex
\subsection{Current package frontier and status of the raw cubic gauge shortcut}
\label{subsec:current-package-frontier-raw-cubic-shortcut}

The local order--\(7\) quotient problem now separates into three distinct
layers.

First, there is a pre-whitening source layer.  At this layer one defines the
tagged two-atom corrected source block and its pair-kernel-linear projection.
This source layer is upstream of whitening, upstream of \(\Phi_K\), and upstream
of determinant or quotient extraction.

Second, there is a matrix corrected-whitening cross-effect layer.  At this
layer one applies
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+):=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}
\]
to the corrected source block and forms the matrix cross-effect before
\(\Phi_K\).

Third, there is a quotient package layer.  At this layer one must define the
pre-determinant fixed-sector coefficient map
\[
C\longmapsto B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C),
\]
and the fixed midpoint quotient class
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C):=[B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C)]
\in \mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]
The quotient layer is not supplied by scalarization or by a raw Fréchet-family
expansion alone.

The current target is therefore not a raw-family cancellation theorem, but the
source-bidegree corrected-whitening quotient-gauge statement
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}\bigl((1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}\bigr)
\in
\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
=
Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}).
\]
Here \(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}\) denotes the actual corrected-whitening package,
not the uncorrected raw cubic Fréchet inventory.
```

## 2. Insert a formal warning that raw UV-026 is rejected

**Placement:** immediately after the subsection above.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Raw seven-family cubic gauge is not the package theorem]
\label{rem:raw-seven-family-cubic-gauge-shortcut-rejected}
A direct source-constrained coefficient audit shows that the raw seven-family
non-\((1,1)\) cubic Fréchet package should not be used as the final quotient
object.  In the notation of the local whitening expansion, the raw inventory is
\[
L_1YR_1,\quad
L_2YR_0,\quad L_0YR_2,\quad
L_2N_0R_1,\quad L_1N_0R_2,\quad
L_3N_0R_0,\quad L_0N_0R_3.
\]
This raw package exhibits many genuine cancellations, including the
source-coefficient cancellation \(Y^5=0\) and the \(Y^6\)-slot left-right
antisymmetry, but source-constrained nonconstant baseline tests leave nonzero
quotient residuals under the provisional fixed-sector quotient line.

Consequently, the following shortcut formulation is not the theorem to prove:
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}\bigl((1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\rm raw}^{(3)}\bigr)
\in
\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]
The correct target must be formulated for the corrected fixed-target package,
including source-bidegree projection, amplitude normalization, and moving
quotient-line transport.
\end{remark}
```

I would keep this as a **remark**, not a theorem, unless you are ready to include the scripts and conventions as an appendix. It captures the finding without overclaiming.

## 3. Define the conditional corrected package representative

**Placement:** after the raw-shortcut warning.

```tex
\begin{definition}[Conditional corrected-whitening package representative]
\label{def:conditional-corrected-whitening-package-representative}
Let
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+):=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}.
\]
Assume the tagged two-atom pre-whitening source block
\[
\mathcal B_2(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)
=
(G_{2,-}^{\corr},N_2^{\corr},G_{2,+}^{\corr})
\]
has been defined at the source level.  Let \(\mathcal B_1(a,h)\) denote the
corresponding one-atom block and let \(\mathcal B_0\) denote the baseline block.

Define the raw corrected-whitening matrix cross-effect before \(\Phi_K\) by
\[
C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)
:=
\mathcal W(\mathcal B_2(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2))
-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_1,h_1))
-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_2,h_2))
+\mathcal W(\mathcal B_0).
\]

Let \(\Pi_{1,1}\) denote the source-bidegree projection onto the exactly
bilinear two-source part, defined before fixed-sector extraction, before
quotient extraction, and before determinant scalarization.  The non-\((1,1)\)
part is
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]

On the collision chart
\[
h_1=h-\frac{\delta}{2},
\qquad
h_2=h+\frac{\delta}{2},
\qquad
a_2=-a_1(1+\kappa\delta),
\]
set
\[
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}
:=
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}(a_1,h-\delta/2;a_2,h+\delta/2),
\]
whenever this expression extends holomorphically to \(\delta=0\).

Let \(A(h):=A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\).  On the good patch
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)\neq0\), let
\[
\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}
\]
denote a representative-level moving-to-midpoint trivialization satisfying
\[
\omega(\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}Z(h),A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m))
=
\omega(Z(h),A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)),
\]
where \(\omega\) is the determinant pairing on
\(\mathfrak f=\operatorname{span}\{I,S\}\).

The conditional fixed-target corrected package representative is
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
:=
\pi_{\mathfrak f}[z^{\le7}]
\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}.
\]
Its order--\(7\) fixed-sector coefficient is
\[
B_{7,\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
:=
[z^7]B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h).
\]
\end{definition}
```

## 4. Add a scope warning immediately after that definition

```tex
\begin{remark}[Scope of Definition~\ref{def:conditional-corrected-whitening-package-representative}]
\label{rem:scope-conditional-corrected-whitening-package}
Definition~\ref{def:conditional-corrected-whitening-package-representative}
is a conditional package definition.  It does not assert:
\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\]
is holomorphic at the collision divisor; it does not assert that
\(\Pi_{1,1}\) is compatible with \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\); and it does not assert
that the non-\((1,1)\) quotient class vanishes.  Those are theorem obligations.

In particular, the tagged pre-whitening source block is not itself the
downstream quotient package.  It supplies source provenance for the corrected
block, while \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\), \(Q_{7,m}^q\), and the non-\((1,1)\)
quotient-gauge theorem remain downstream targets.
\end{remark}
```

This tracks the existing UV-025 guardrails: the tagged source block is safe only as pre-whitening source data, not as quotient package closure. turn80file2

## 5. Add the quotient-line connection lemma

**Placement:** after the package representative definition.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Fixed-target quotient-line connection]
\label{lem:fixed-target-quotient-line-connection}
Let
\[
A(h)=A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=\sum_{i\ge0}(h-m)^iA_i
\]
and let
\[
B(h)=\sum_{j\ge0}(h-m)^jB_j
\]
be a moving fixed-sector representative.  The moving quotient determinant is
\[
\omega(B(h),A(h)),
\]
whereas the raw fixed-target determinant is
\[
\omega(B(h),A_0).
\]
Thus the connection correction is
\[
\omega(B(h),A(h))-\omega(B(h),A_0)
=
\omega(B(h),A(h)-A_0).
\]
Consequently, in coefficient-normalized Taylor convention,
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},k}
=
\sum_{i=1}^{k}\omega(B_{k-i},A_i).
\]
If derivative-normalized coefficients are used instead, then
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},k}
=
\sum_{i=1}^{k}\binom{k}{i}\omega(B_{k-i},A_i).
\]
\end{lemma}
```

## 6. Define the lower moving representatives needed by the agents

```tex
\begin{definition}[Lower moving representatives in a source monomial]
\label{def:lower-moving-representatives-source-monomial}
Let \(M\) be a source monomial in the corrected package representative
\(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)\).  Write
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)\big|_M
=
\sum_{j\ge0}(h-m)^jB_j[M].
\]
For the residual monomials
\[
M_A:=\dot r_3r_2\dot r_4,
\qquad
M_C:=\dot r_3\dot r_2r_4,
\]
the lower moving representatives are
\[
B_j[M_A],\qquad B_j[M_C],
\qquad j=0,\ldots,6.
\]
The connection correction at order \(7\) is
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M_A]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega(B_{7-i}[M_A],A_i),
\]
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M_C]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega(B_{7-i}[M_C],A_i),
\]
with binomial weights in derivative-normalized convention.
\end{definition}
```

## 7. State the corrected UV-026 theorem target

```tex
\begin{conjecture}[Corrected non-\((1,1)\) quotient gauge]
\label{conj:corrected-non-11-quotient-gauge}
For the actual corrected package representative of
Definition~\ref{def:conditional-corrected-whitening-package-representative},
one has
\[
B_{7,\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}
\bigl((1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}\bigr)
\in
\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)
\]
in the fixed midpoint quotient, after amplitude normalization and
moving-to-midpoint quotient-line transport.
Equivalently,
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
=
Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}).
\]
\end{conjecture}
```

Use `conjecture`, `target`, or `worktarget` depending on your draft style. Do **not** make this a theorem yet.

## 8. Add a “what is established / what remains” remark

```tex
\begin{remark}[Established raw-package failure and remaining package task]
\label{rem:established-raw-package-failure-remaining-package-task}
The source-constrained raw seven-family calculation should be used as a
negative guide.  It shows that the raw cubic Fréchet inventory is not the
correct quotient package object.  In particular, the shortcut claim
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}\bigl((1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\rm raw}^{(3)}\bigr)
\in
\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)
\]
is not a safe theorem statement.

The remaining task is to construct and test the corrected package
representative \(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)\).  For the first surviving
source monomials
\[
M_A=\dot r_3r_2\dot r_4,
\qquad
M_C=\dot r_3\dot r_2r_4,
\]
the connection-corrected tests are
\[
R_7^{(A)}
+
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega(B_{7-i}[M_A],A_i)
=0,
\]
\[
R_7^{(C)}
+
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega(B_{7-i}[M_C],A_i)
=0.
\]
The required data are the moving line coefficients \(A_i\) and the lower moving
representatives \(B_j[M_A],B_j[M_C]\).  These are package-level data, not raw
seven-family coefficients.
\end{remark}
```

## 9. Add an agent-facing status block

This is not traditional paper prose, but I would include it as a “Working frontier” remark or appendix note so agents stop rediscovering old failures.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Working frontier for subsequent coefficient agents]
\label{rem:working-frontier-for-agents}
The following shortcuts are now disallowed.

\begin{enumerate}
\item Do not prove quotient invisibility by \(\Phi_K\)-scalar hiding.
\item Do not use determinant scalarization as the definition of the quotient.
\item Do not replace the corrected package by the raw seven-family Fréchet
inventory.
\item Do not substitute raw seven-family lower coefficients for the lower
moving representatives \(B_j[M]\).
\item Do not treat the tagged pre-whitening source block as a downstream
quotient package.
\end{enumerate}

The current legitimate objects are:
\[
\mathcal B_2
\quad\text{at the tagged pre-whitening source level,}
\]
\[
C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\quad\text{as a matrix cross-effect before \(\Phi_K\),}
\]
and the conditional corrected package representative
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
=
\pi_{\mathfrak f}[z^{\le7}]
\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}
\left(
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}(1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\right).
\]
The next computable target is the lower moving table
\[
A_i,\quad B_j[\dot r_3r_2\dot r_4],\quad
B_j[\dot r_3\dot r_2r_4].
\]
\end{remark}
```

# Summary of the update

This update will give all agents the right context:

1. **UV-025:** safe as a guarded tagged pre-whitening source-block definition, not a quotient theorem.  
2. **UV-024:** still needs the actual pre-determinant coefficient map \(C\mapsto B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C)\).  
3. **UV-026 old form:** raw seven-family cubic gauge shortcut is rejected by source-constrained tests.  
4. **UV-026 new form:** corrected package quotient gauge, with source projection, amplitude normalization, midpoint trivialization, and connection correction.  
5. **Next gap:** define \(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)\) strongly enough to compute lower moving representatives \(B_0,\ldots,B_6\).

This is the right paper reset. It prevents future agents from wasting cycles on raw-family cancellation and points them at the actual missing object.

## Assistant (2026-04-24 16:43:29)

_Thought for 21s_

Yes. The paper needs **both** layers:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Package-definition layer}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite Fréchet/Sylvester coefficient layer}.
}
\]

They serve different purposes. The package layer says **what object is being tested**. The Fréchet/Sylvester layer says **how its raw/local coefficients are computed**. Without the Fréchet definitions and expansions, future agents will keep rediscovering the same coefficient tables, sign conventions, and false shortcuts.

The current source already records that the raw coefficient tables are derivable as finite recurrences, but that those recurrences do not themselves prove quotient gauge. That distinction should be explicit in the paper. 

## Recommended paper structure

I would add a new section or appendix:

```tex
\section{Finite Fréchet coefficient calculus for the corrected-whitening block}
\label{sec:finite-frechet-coefficient-calculus}
```

This section should sit **before** the corrected package frontier, or as an appendix referenced from that section.

The structure should be:

1. **Source-linear same-point primitives**
2. **Baseline mixed block**
3. **First Fréchet/Sylvester recursion**
4. **Second Fréchet/Sylvester recursion**
5. **Third Fréchet/Sylvester recursion**
6. **Midpoint parity**
7. **Fixed-sector projection rules**
8. **Three-/five-/seven-family coefficient gates**
9. **Raw-package failure status**

The raw seven-family failure should then point forward to the corrected package definition, rather than being buried in a coefficient subsection.

# 1. Add source-linear same-point primitive table

This captures Agent 1’s \(X_{\varepsilon,n}^a\) table. The table is source-constrained: \(U,E,G,V\) have already been reduced to the residual tower \(r_\rho\), not free kernels. 

```tex
\subsection{Source-linear same-point primitives}
\label{subsec:source-linear-same-point-primitives}

For \(\varepsilon\in\{-,+\}\), identified with \(\varepsilon=-1,+1\), write
\[
X_{\varepsilon,n}
:=
[z^n]\mathfrak D_Q(\delta G_\varepsilon^{\lin})
=
\sum_{a=2}^{6}X_{\varepsilon,n}^a r_a.
\]
For \(n\le2\), the nonzero source-constrained coefficients are
\[
X_{\varepsilon,0}^2=\frac{1}{24\pi}(I-D),
\]
\[
X_{\varepsilon,1}^2=\frac{\varepsilon}{4\pi Q}S,
\qquad
X_{\varepsilon,1}^3=\frac{\varepsilon}{48\pi Q^2}(I-D),
\]
\[
X_{\varepsilon,2}^2
=
\left(
\frac{1}{8\pi Q^2}
+
\frac{q^2}{32\pi Q^4}
\right)I
+
\left(
\frac{1}{8\pi Q^2}
-
\frac{q^2}{32\pi Q^4}
\right)D,
\]
\[
X_{\varepsilon,2}^3=\frac{1}{16\pi Q^3}S,
\qquad
X_{\varepsilon,2}^4=\frac{1}{192\pi Q^4}(I-D).
\]
All other \(X_{\varepsilon,n}^a\) with \(n\le2\), \(2\le a\le6\), vanish.
```

Add a remark:

```tex
\begin{remark}
These coefficients are not free symbolic inputs.  They are the residual-source
normal form of the source-linear same-point perturbation.
\end{remark}
```

---

# 2. Add baseline mixed block \(N^{(0)}\)

This is required for the \(N_0\)-middle five-family gates. It should not be omitted. The current finite table says the five-family audit requires \(N^{(0)}_{0..4}\). 

```tex
\subsection{Baseline mixed block coefficients}
\label{subsec:baseline-mixed-block-coefficients}

Let
\[
s=\frac{z}{Q^2},\qquad
q_\pm(z)=q_0\left(m\pm\frac{z}{2Q^2}\right),
\]
\[
\Delta_0(z)=\Phi_0(t_-)-\Phi_0(t_+),
\qquad
C(z)=\cos\Delta_0(z),\quad S(z)=\sin\Delta_0(z).
\]
The baseline mixed block is
\[
N^{(0)}(z)=\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
-\dfrac{2S}{s}
&
\dfrac{S+q_+sC}{s^2}
\\[1.4ex]
-\dfrac{S+q_-sC}{s^2}
&
\dfrac{(q_-+q_+)sC+(2-q_-q_+s^2)S}{2s^3}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
For \(j\ge0\), write
\[
N^{(0)}_j=[z^j]N^{(0)}(z).
\]
Then \(N^{(0)}_{0..4}\) are obtained entrywise from the coefficient formulas
\[
(N^{(0)}_j)_{11}
=
-\frac{2Q^2}{\pi}[z^{j+1}]S(z),
\]
\[
(N^{(0)}_j)_{12}
=
\frac{Q^4}{\pi}
\left(
[z^{j+2}]S(z)
+
\frac1{Q^2}[z^{j+1}](q_+(z)C(z))
\right),
\]
\[
(N^{(0)}_j)_{21}
=
-\frac{Q^4}{\pi}
\left(
[z^{j+2}]S(z)
+
\frac1{Q^2}[z^{j+1}](q_-(z)C(z))
\right),
\]
\[
(N^{(0)}_j)_{22}
=
\frac{Q^6}{2\pi}
\left(
\frac1{Q^2}[z^{j+2}]((q_-+q_+)C)
+
2[z^{j+3}]S
-
\frac1{Q^4}[z^{j+1}](q_-q_+S)
\right).
\]
```

---

# 3. Add first Fréchet/Sylvester recursion

This prevents confusion about \(\Lambda\)-tables and orders. 

```tex
\subsection{First Fréchet coefficient table}
\label{subsec:first-frechet-coefficient-table}

Let \(P_{\varepsilon,0}\) be the zeroth square-root coefficient of
\(G_\varepsilon^{(0)}\), and let \(\Sigma_\varepsilon(C)\) denote the unique
solution of
\[
P_{\varepsilon,0}Z+ZP_{\varepsilon,0}=C.
\]
For each source index \(a\), define \(S_{\varepsilon,n}^a\) recursively by
\[
S_{\varepsilon,n}^a
=
\Sigma_\varepsilon
\left(
X_{\varepsilon,n}^a
-
\sum_{j=1}^{n}
\bigl(
P_{\varepsilon,j}S_{\varepsilon,n-j}^a
+
S_{\varepsilon,n-j}^aP_{\varepsilon,j}
\bigr)
\right).
\]
Then
\[
\Lambda_{\varepsilon,n}^{a}
=
-\sum_{i+j+k=n}
W_{\varepsilon,i}S_{\varepsilon,j}^aW_{\varepsilon,k}.
\]
The required ranges used below include
\[
\Lambda^-_{2,0..4},\qquad
\Lambda^-_{3,1..5},\qquad
\Lambda^-_{4,2..6}.
\]
```

---

# 4. Add second Fréchet/Sylvester recursion

This is essential because most surviving gates use \(H^-_{ab,n}\). 

```tex
\subsection{Second Fréchet coefficient table}
\label{subsec:second-frechet-coefficient-table}

For an ordered source pair \((a,b)\), define \(T_{\varepsilon,n}^{ab}\) by
\[
T_{\varepsilon,n}^{ab}
=
\Sigma_\varepsilon
\left(
-\sum_{i+j=n}
(S_{\varepsilon,i}^aS_{\varepsilon,j}^b+
 S_{\varepsilon,i}^bS_{\varepsilon,j}^a)
-
\sum_{j=1}^{n}
(P_{\varepsilon,j}T_{\varepsilon,n-j}^{ab}
+
T_{\varepsilon,n-j}^{ab}P_{\varepsilon,j})
\right).
\]
The corresponding second Fréchet coefficient is
\[
H_{\varepsilon,n}^{ab}
=
\sum_{i+j+k+\ell+m=n}
\left(
W_{\varepsilon,i}S_{\varepsilon,j}^bW_{\varepsilon,k}
S_{\varepsilon,\ell}^aW_{\varepsilon,m}
+
W_{\varepsilon,i}S_{\varepsilon,j}^aW_{\varepsilon,k}
S_{\varepsilon,\ell}^bW_{\varepsilon,m}
\right)
-
\sum_{i+j+k=n}
W_{\varepsilon,i}T_{\varepsilon,j}^{ab}W_{\varepsilon,k}.
\]
The five-family endpoint gate uses
\[
H^-_{32,1..5},\qquad
H^-_{34,3..7},\qquad
H^-_{24,2..6}.
\]
```

---

# 5. Add third Fréchet/Sylvester recursion

This one is not fully present yet, but it must be added because the seven-family audit needs \(L_3N_0R_0/L_0N_0R_3\). Agents should not improvise it each time.

```tex
\subsection{Third Fréchet coefficient table}
\label{subsec:third-frechet-coefficient-table}

For source triple \((a,b,c)\), define \(U_{\varepsilon,n}^{abc}\) by the third
Sylvester recursion
\[
P_{\varepsilon,0}U_{\varepsilon,n}^{abc}
+
U_{\varepsilon,n}^{abc}P_{\varepsilon,0}
=
-\sum_{i+j=n}
\Bigl(
S_{\varepsilon,i}^{a}T_{\varepsilon,j}^{bc}
+S_{\varepsilon,i}^{b}T_{\varepsilon,j}^{ac}
+S_{\varepsilon,i}^{c}T_{\varepsilon,j}^{ab}
+
T_{\varepsilon,i}^{bc}S_{\varepsilon,j}^{a}
+T_{\varepsilon,i}^{ac}S_{\varepsilon,j}^{b}
+T_{\varepsilon,i}^{ab}S_{\varepsilon,j}^{c}
\Bigr)
-
\sum_{j=1}^{n}
\bigl(
P_{\varepsilon,j}U_{\varepsilon,n-j}^{abc}
+
U_{\varepsilon,n-j}^{abc}P_{\varepsilon,j}
\bigr).
\]
The all-left third Fréchet coefficient \(G^-_{abc,n}\) is then obtained from
the coefficient of the cubic source monomial in
\[
(G_-^{(0)}+\text{source})^{-1/2},
\]
equivalently by the coefficient recursion determined by \(U_{\varepsilon,n}^{abc}\),
the lower \(S\)- and \(T\)-coefficients, and the inverse coefficients
\(W_{\varepsilon,n}\).

The seven-family audit for the first surviving residual requires
\[
G^-_{324,3..7}
\quad\text{and}\quad
G^+_{324,3..7}.
\]
```

This can be refined later into the exact coefficient formula for \(G\), but even adding the recursion/target range now is valuable.

---

# 6. Add midpoint parity lemma

This closed the \(Y^6\) slot, so it should be in the source. 

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Midpoint parity of Fréchet coefficients]
\label{lem:midpoint-parity-frechet-coefficients}
In the midpoint chart,
\[
t_\pm=m\pm\frac{z}{2Q^2},
\]
the plus-side and minus-side coefficients satisfy
\[
\Lambda^+_{a,n}=(-1)^n\Lambda^-_{a,n},
\]
and
\[
H^+_{ab,n}=(-1)^nH^-_{ab,n},
\]
for the source indices used in the finite coefficient tables.  The same
parity convention applies to the third Fréchet coefficients once the
corresponding \(G^\pm_{abc,n}\) table is defined.
\end{lemma}
```

---

# 7. Add fixed-sector \(J\)-projection multiplication rule

This rule explains the \(Y^6\) cancellation and the \(HJW\)-residuals. It needs to be in the paper so agents stop recomputing it. 

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Fixed-sector projection of a \(J\)-middle product]
\label{lem:fixed-sector-projection-j-middle-product}
Let
\[
H=aI+bS+cD,\qquad W=pI+qS+rD.
\]
Then
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(HJW)
=
(cq-br)I+(cp-ar)S,
\]
and
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(WJH)
=
-(cq-br)I-(cp-ar)S.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(WJH)=-\pi_{\mathfrak f}(HJW).
\]
\end{lemma}
```

---

# 8. Add finite raw-gate summaries

These should go in a “Computed finite gates” subsection.

## \(Y\)-slot facts

```tex
\begin{remark}[Source-coefficient parity in the \(Y\)-slot]
\label{rem:y-slot-source-coefficient-parity}
For the grade-five mixed slot \(Y^a\), the source-coefficient table gives
\[
Y^5=0,
\]
while
\[
Y^6=y_6J,\qquad y_6=\frac{1}{53760\pi Q^9}.
\]
Thus the \(Y^5\)-slot residuals vanish individually, and the \(Y^6\)-slot
residuals cancel in left-right pairs by
Lemma~\ref{lem:fixed-sector-projection-j-middle-product}.
\end{remark}
```

## First invisible endpoint residual

```tex
\begin{remark}[First invisible endpoint residual]
\label{rem:first-invisible-endpoint-residual}
For the invisible endpoint pair \((3,4)\) with middle source \(q=2\), the
three-family \(C_2\) gate reduces to one scalar
\[
d^-_{324}
=
y_4\Xi_{324},
\qquad
y_4=-\frac{q^2}{640\pi Q^9},
\]
where
\[
\Xi_{324}
=
\mathcal D(H^-_{2;32}JW_0-H^-_{1;32}JW_1).
\]
The accompanying \(d^+_{324}\) term vanishes by \(Y^3=0\), and the
\(d^+_{234}\) term vanishes by source-degree support
\(H^-_{0..2;34}=0\).
\end{remark}
```

## Five-family \(N^{(0)}\)-weighted gate

```tex
\begin{remark}[Five-family \(N^{(0)}\)-weighted endpoint gate]
\label{rem:five-family-n0-weighted-endpoint-gate}
The \(N_0\)-middle families do not collapse to the \(r+t=2\) range.  They
involve the full baseline middle factor and hence the convolution
\[
n+s+t=7.
\]
For the first endpoint residual define
\[
E^-_{324}
=
\mathcal D
\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le5\\2\le t\le6\\n+t\le7}}
(-1)^tH^-_{32,n}N^{(0)}_{7-n-t}\Lambda^-_{4,t},
\]
\[
E^+_{234}
=
\mathcal D
\sum_{\substack{0\le n\le4\\3\le t\le7\\n+t\le7}}
(-1)^t\Lambda^-_{2,n}N^{(0)}_{7-n-t}H^-_{34,t},
\]
\[
E^+_{324}
=
\mathcal D
\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le5\\2\le t\le6\\n+t\le7}}
(-1)^t\Lambda^-_{3,n}N^{(0)}_{7-n-t}H^-_{24,t}.
\]
The five-family residuals are
\[
R^{(A)}_5=2y_4\Xi_{324}+2E^-_{324}+E^+_{234},
\]
\[
R^{(C)}_5=y_4\Xi_{324}+E^-_{324}+2E^+_{324}.
\]
These are finite coefficient identities, not consequences of support or
generic Fréchet algebra.
\end{remark}
```

This exactly matches the finite recurrence/gate status. turn81file1

# 9. How to present numeric/computational negative results

Do **not** put the raw scripts in the theorem body. Put them in an appendix or a remark:

```tex
\begin{remark}[Computational status of the raw package]
\label{rem:computational-status-raw-package}
Source-constrained nonconstant baseline tests show that the raw five-family and
raw seven-family packages are not generally quotient-gauge under the provisional
fixed-sector line.  The constant baseline is quotient-inconclusive because
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}=0\).  Nonconstant linear baselines give nonzero determinant
residuals.  These tests reject the raw seven-family shortcut, but they do not
test the corrected package representative
\(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)\), whose lower moving coefficients
\(B_j[M]\) remain undefined.
\end{remark}
```

This keeps the result honest: it rejects the shortcut, not the intended corrected package theorem. turn82file14

---

# Bottom line

Yes, the missing Fréchet definitions and expansions are absolutely needed.

But they should be placed in a **finite coefficient calculus section**, separate from the **corrected package definition section**.

The paper should now have two complementary additions:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Finite Fréchet coefficient calculus}
}
\]

for \(X,S,\Lambda,T,H,G,N^{(0)},Y\), parity, and raw gates;

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{Corrected package representative}
}
\]

for \(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr},\Pi_{1,1},-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2},\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m},B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}\), and \(B_j[M]\).

The first prevents future coefficient confusion. The second defines the actual theorem object.
## User (2026-04-24 16:45:49)

here are the proposed package definitions from both agent 1 and 2

## Assistant (2026-04-24 16:45:58)

_Thought for 8s_

Yes. Both agents are broadly aligned, and the definition layer is now close to paper-ready. I would merge them, but with a few corrections.

## Main assessment

Agent 1’s version is stronger on **representative-level midpoint trivialization**: it correctly notes that determinant preservation fixes only the quotient coordinate, not a unique representative, so it introduces a transverse section \(E_m\) and a gauge functional \(\ell_h\). That is important if the paper wants \(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)\) as an actual representative, not merely a quotient class. L65-L98

Agent 2’s version is cleaner on **the theorem-obligation separation**: it clearly separates definitions from edge divisibility, source-bidegree compatibility, holomorphic extension, connection cancellation, and corrected UV-026. That is exactly the architecture we need. L127-L184

I would merge them into one consolidated section with these principles:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Define the raw matrix cross-effect first.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Project source-bidegree before quotient extraction.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Apply amplitude normalization conditionally.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Define a representative-level midpoint trivialization.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Only then define }B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h), B_j[M], Q_{7,m}^q.
}
\]

## Important correction

The notation

\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})F
\]

should be defined carefully. Agent 1 wrote:

\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})C
:=
\sum_{\substack{p,q\ge1\\(p,q)\ne(1,1)}} C_{p,q}.
\]

That keeps only mixed terms with both \(p,q\ge1\). This is probably what we want for the **mixed source cross-effect**, because pure one-source terms should already be removed by the cross-effect. But if the cross-effect already kills \(p=0\) or \(q=0\), this is fine; if not, define it as:

\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}C
:=
\sum_{\substack{p,q\ge1\\(p,q)\ne(1,1)}} C_{p,q}.
\]

Use a subscript like \((1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}\) to avoid ambiguity.

## Consolidated LaTeX insertion

I would insert the following as a new section.

```tex
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
% Corrected quotient package representative
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

\section{Corrected fixed-target quotient package}
\label{sec:corrected-fixed-target-quotient-package}

\begin{definition}[Raw corrected-whitening matrix cross-effect before \(\Phi_K\)]
\label{def:raw-corrected-whitening-cross-effect}
Let
\[
\mathcal B_0=(G_-^{(0)},N^{(0)},G_+^{(0)})
\]
be the baseline corrected block, and let
\[
\mathcal B_1(a_i,h_i)
\]
denote the one-source tagged corrected block obtained by substituting only the
\(i\)-th tagged source perturbation into the corrected same-point and mixed
block formulae.  Let
\[
\mathcal B_2(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)
\]
be the tagged two-source corrected block.

Let
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+):=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}
\]
be the corrected-whitening transfer, defined on the good analytic patch where
the inverse square roots are holomorphic.

Define the matrix cross-effect before applying \(\Phi_K\), determinant
scalarization, or quotient extraction by
\[
\begin{aligned}
C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)
:={}&
\mathcal W(\mathcal B_2(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2))
-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_1,h_1))
\\
&-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_2,h_2))
+\mathcal W(\mathcal B_0).
\end{aligned}
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Source-bidegree projection before quotient extraction]
\label{def:source-bidegree-projection-before-quotient}
Work with formal source tags \(\tau_1,\tau_2\) retained through the matrix
cross-effect.  For a tagged matrix series
\[
F(\tau_1,\tau_2)=\sum_{p,q\ge0}\tau_1^p\tau_2^qF_{p,q},
\]
define
\[
\Pi_{1,1}F:=F_{1,1}.
\]
The non-\((1,1)\) mixed source component is
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}F
:=
\sum_{\substack{p,q\ge1\\(p,q)\ne(1,1)}}F_{p,q}.
\]
This projection is applied before fixed-sector extraction, before
\(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\), before determinant pairing, and before quotienting.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Amplitude-normalized non-\((1,1)\) package]
\label{def:amplitude-normalized-non-11-package}
On the collision chart
\[
h_1=h-\frac{\delta}{2},
\qquad
h_2=h+\frac{\delta}{2},
\qquad
a_2=-a_1(1+\kappa\delta),
\]
define the formally amplitude-normalized package by
\[
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}
:=
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}
C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\!\left(a_1,h-\frac{\delta}{2};
        -a_1(1+\kappa\delta),h+\frac{\delta}{2}\right).
\]
This is a matrix-valued object before \(\Phi_K\), before determinant
scalarization, and before quotient extraction.  Its use below is conditional
on holomorphic extension to \(\delta=0\).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Representative-level midpoint trivialization]
\label{def:representative-level-midpoint-trivialization}
Let
\[
A(h):=A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\in\mathfrak f_h
\]
be the moving quintic fixed-sector line.  Fix a good patch on which
\[
A(m)\ne0.
\]
Fix an analytic identification
\[
\tau_{h\to m}:\mathfrak f_h\longrightarrow\mathfrak f_m.
\]
Write
\[
A_h^m:=\tau_{h\to m}A(h),
\qquad
A_m:=A(m).
\]
Let
\[
\omega_m:\mathfrak f_m\times\mathfrak f_m\to\mathbf C
\]
be the fixed determinant form in the \(I,S\)-basis.

Choose a local transverse section \(E_m\in\mathfrak f_m\) satisfying
\[
\omega_m(E_m,A_m)=1,
\]
and an analytic linear functional
\[
\ell_h:\mathfrak f_m\to\mathbf C
\]
satisfying
\[
\ell_h(A_h^m)=1.
\]
For \(Z(h)\in\mathfrak f_h\), set
\[
\widetilde Z(h):=\tau_{h\to m}Z(h),
\]
and define
\[
\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}Z(h)
:=
\ell_h(\widetilde Z(h))A_m
+
\omega_m(\widetilde Z(h),A_h^m)E_m.
\]
Then
\[
\omega_m\!\left(\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}Z(h),A_m\right)
=
\omega_m(\widetilde Z(h),A_h^m).
\]
Changing \(E_m\) or \(\ell_h\) changes the representative only by a multiple of
\(A_m\), and hence does not change its class in
\[
\mathfrak f_m/\mathbf C A_m.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Normalized corrected-whitening package representative]
\label{def:normalized-corrected-whitening-package-representative}
Assume the amplitude-normalized package
\[
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}
\]
extends holomorphically to \(\delta=0\).  Define the normalized fixed-target
corrected-whitening package by
\[
C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}(h)
:=
\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}
\left(
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}(h)
\right).
\]
Define its moving fixed-sector representative through order \(7\) by
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
:=
\pi_{\mathfrak f_m}[z^{\le7}]\,C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}(h).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
=
\sum_{j=0}^{7}(h-m)^jB_j+O((h-m)^8),
\qquad
B_j\in\mathfrak f_m.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Package-level order-\(7\) quotient extractor]
\label{def:package-level-order-7-quotient-extractor}
For the normalized package representative, define
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
:=
[z^7]\,B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]
The quotient-valued order-\(7\) extractor is
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
:=
\left[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
\right]
\in
\mathfrak f_m/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Lower moving package coefficients]
\label{def:lower-moving-package-coefficients}
Let \(M\) be a residual-source monomial.  Define
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h;M)
\]
to be the coefficient of \(M\) in \(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)\).
For
\[
M_A:=\dot r_3r_2\dot r_4,
\qquad
M_C:=\dot r_3\dot r_2r_4,
\]
write
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h;M)
=
\sum_{j\ge0}(h-m)^jB_j[M].
\]
Thus
\[
B_j[M]:=[(h-m)^jM]B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h),
\qquad
0\le j\le6.
\]

If
\[
B_j[M]=u_j^B[M]I+v_j^B[M]S
\]
and
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=\sum_{i\ge0}(h-m)^iA_i,
\qquad
A_i=u_i^AI+v_i^AS,
\]
then the order-\(7\) quotient-line connection correction on \(M\) is
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}
\omega_m(B_{7-i}[M],A_i).
\]
In coordinates,
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}
\left(
u_{7-i}^B[M]v_i^A
-
u_i^Av_{7-i}^B[M]
\right).
\]
If \(A_i,B_j\) are derivative-normalized rather than coefficient-normalized,
replace this formula by
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\binom{7}{i}
\omega_m(B_{7-i}[M],A_i).
\]
\end{definition}
```

## The theorem-obligation block to add after the definitions

```tex
\begin{remark}[Definition-level material and theorem obligations]
\label{rem:definition-level-material-and-theorem-obligations}
Definitions~\ref{def:raw-corrected-whitening-cross-effect}--
\ref{def:lower-moving-package-coefficients} specify the intended corrected
package object.  They do not prove its quotient-gauge property.

The following are theorem obligations.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Edge divisibility.}
One must prove
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
\]
on the collision chart, so that
\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\]
extends holomorphically to \(\delta=0\).

\item \textbf{Source-bidegree compatibility.}
The source-bidegree projector must be compatible with the pre-determinant
fixed-sector coefficient extraction.  It is not valid to define
\(\Pi_{1,1}\) after determinant scalarization or after quotienting.

\item \textbf{Midpoint trivialization independence.}
The quotient class in
\[
\mathfrak f_m/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)
\]
must be independent of the auxiliary representative choices \(E_m,\ell_h\).

\item \textbf{Connection cancellation.}
For the residual monomials
\[
M_A=\dot r_3r_2\dot r_4,
\qquad
M_C=\dot r_3\dot r_2r_4,
\]
one must prove
\[
R_7^{(A)}
+
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega_m(B_{7-i}[M_A],A_i)
=
0,
\]
and
\[
R_7^{(C)}
+
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega_m(B_{7-i}[M_C],A_i)
=
0.
\]

\item \textbf{Corrected UV-026.}
The final corrected quotient-gauge statement is
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}
\left(
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}
\right)
\in
\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m),
\]
equivalently
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
=
Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}).
\]
\end{enumerate}
\end{remark}
```

## One issue to flag

The notation

\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
=
\pi_{\mathfrak f_m}[z^{\le7}]C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}(h)
\]

is a little awkward because after \(\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}\), the object is already in the midpoint fiber \(\mathfrak f_m\). That is fine, but do **not** write \(\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\) after trivialization. Agent 1’s definition sometimes uses \(\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\) before the fixed-target transport and \(\pi_{\mathfrak f_m}\) after. The consolidated version above always uses \(\pi_{\mathfrak f_m}\) after transport, which is cleaner.

Use Agent 1’s representative-level trivialization, Agent 2’s theorem-obligation separation, and the consolidated notation above.

Yes. The clean way to state it is:

**It does not directly “prove RH by solving 2-point and 4-point mixed.”**  
It solves them as *independent mixed-geometry frontiers* by reducing both to the same corrected source/provenance package.

**For the 2-point mixed branch**

The draft already says the exact remaining 2-point source input is finite and concrete: for the actual corrected two-atom cubic/quintic/septic package, prove:

```tex
swap symmetry,
one-amplitude collapse,
diagonal merger
\mathfrak P_2(a_1,h;a_2,h)=(a_1+a_2)F_h.
```

Once those hold, the quadratic factorization is formal:

```tex
\mathcal I_2=a_1a_2(h_1-h_2)^2\mathcal J_2.
```

So the current UV-022/023/024/026 push is aimed exactly at supplying the missing corrected package behind that statement:

- UV-025 gives the tagged pre-whitening two-atom source block.
- UV-022 asks for exact source-weight-linear one-amplitude collapse.
- UV-023 asks for quotient-invisibility of higher transfer terms through order 7.
- UV-024 defines the actual pre-determinant fixed-sector order-7 extractor `B_7^{\mathfrak f}` and quotient class `Q_{7,m}^q`.
- UV-026 kills or gauges the non-`(1,1)` cubic finite-order source terms.

If that chain closes, then the actual order-7 quotient defect is no longer an uncontrolled two-atom mixed object. It becomes the intended quotient edge package `\mathcal H_7^q`, with the non-`(1,1)` garbage invisible modulo `\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)`.

Then the 2-point mixed obstruction reduces as follows:

```tex
corrected two-atom package
→ source-linear cross-effect
→ B_7^{\mathfrak f}
→ quotient class Q_{7,m}^q
→ midpoint quotient edge package \mathcal H_7^q
→ reduced obstruction M/c on good patches.
```

On compact good patches with `c != 0` and `M != 0`, that excludes close cancelling exact two-point bad cores. On the residual fixed-shear corner, the draft already shows finite parity-normalized transport descends to `u=t^2`, has rank-one state on the quartic/sextic rung, and has no finite hidden reset. The remaining question is whether the actual corrected package is state-local in that descended quotient state. The UV chain is exactly the attempt to prove that by provenance, rather than by scalar quotient handwaving.

**For the 4-point mixed branch**

The draft’s mixed 4-point result is not “nothing remains anywhere”; it is sharper:

```tex
a genuinely mixed clean 4-point branch cannot persist independently
inside {T_v != 0} ∩ {\mathcal R != 0}.
```

So every clean mixed branch is forced into one of the scalar exits:

```tex
T_v = 0
```

or

```tex
\mathcal R = 0.
```

Here `T_v=0` is the centered/mixed scalar exit, and `\mathcal R=0` is the nonmixed affine-planar scalar exit. That means mixed 4-point geometry is no longer an independent carrier problem once source-coupled and patch-loss walls are absorbed.

The current UV package matters because the dangerous mixed 4-point cases are controlled by the same pair-level fixed-sector object:

```tex
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(s)=u_5(s)I+v_5(s)S.
```

The mixed low-order patterns reduce to finite Wronskian-ratio/order data, but identities involving only

```tex
(c, v_5, u_5, \Delta_7)
```

cannot eliminate them in general. That is why the proof needs the stronger pair-level source package: it must show that the actual corrected cross-effect contributes no independent non-scalar fixed-sector direction.

That is exactly UV-026’s role. If every non-`(1,1)` cubic source term satisfies

```tex
B_7^{\mathfrak f}(T) \in \mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m),
```

then those cubic mixed terms do not create a new quotient direction. They are gauge along the already-known `A_5^{\mathfrak f}` line. So the mixed 4-point branch has no new local mixed carrier beyond the scalar exits already listed in the paper.

The key implication is:

```tex
UV-026 non-(1,1) gauge
→ no extra fixed-sector cubic direction
→ mixed 4-point source-coupled walls cannot carry an independent clean branch
→ global mixed-to-scalar capture applies
→ branch exits through T_v=0 or \mathcal R=0.
```

**Current honest status**

The Y-slot part of UV-026 now supports this mechanism under homogeneous scalar grade: `L_1YR_1`, `L_2YR_0`, and `L_0YR_2` are absent at `B_7`.

The mechanism is not fully closed yet because the non-Y gates remain:

```tex
L_2N_0R_1 / L_1N_0R_2
L_3N_0R_0 / L_0N_0R_3
```

So the exact statement is:

The push has made explicit how the same source-provenance theorem would close the residual 2-point mixed package and remove mixed 4-point as an independent branch. It has not yet finished that theorem; the remaining work is the leading-coefficient fixed-sector test for the live non-Y cubic gates, plus baseline `q_0` and grade-0 handling.

## Summary of additional edits

This should explain that the 2-point and mixed 4-point frontiers are not being solved separately. They both reduce to the same corrected source/provenance package:

\[
\mathcal B_2
\to C_{\mathcal W}
\to B_7^{\mathfrak f}
\to Q_{7,m}^q
\to \mathcal H_7^q.

### 2. Add a conditional corrected package definition layer

Use the merged definitions from Agents 1 and 2: raw corrected-whitening cross-effect, pre-quotient \(\Pi_{1,1}\), amplitude normalization, midpoint trivialization, \(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}\), \(Q_{7,m}^q\), and lower \(B_j[M]\). The current source says \(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}\) is definable as a target schema, but not yet a computable object because the normalized corrected package representative is not fully defined. turn86file5

### 3. Add a finite Fréchet coefficient calculus section

This is necessary. The package section defines **what object** is being tested; the Fréchet/Sylvester section defines **how raw/local coefficients are computed**. Include \(X_{\varepsilon,n}^a\), \(N^{(0)}_j\), \(S^a_n\), \(\Lambda^a_n\), \(T^{ab}_n\), \(H^{ab}_n\), and a third-Fréchet table placeholder. The latest UV ledger explicitly asks for finite normalized source tables and first/second/third Fréchet tables derived from \(S^2=G\) and \(BS=I\). 

### 4. Add a scoped homogeneous-grade UV-026 status lemma

Under

\[
\operatorname{Gr}_a r=\frac{r^{(a+2)}(m)}{(a+2)!}(t-m)^{a+2},
\]

the \(Y\)-slot families

\[
L_1YR_1,\qquad L_2YR_0,\qquad L_0YR_2

are absent at \(B_7\). This should be a scoped lemma, not a determinant-gauge theorem. The first live non-\(Y\) gate is \(L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2\), and it requires only leading coefficients because \(N_0[0]\) gives exact order \(7\). turn86file6

### 5. Add a “quarantined alternatives / negative shortcuts” remark

This should explicitly say that matrix-output \(M^{[5]}\) or parity-corrected non-homogeneous grade conventions are not the active theorem. If revived, they reopen the \(Y\)-slot gates and require a no-double-counting/source-inventory theorem. The latest findings record exactly this caveat.

This is the missing holistic view from the other agent. The corrected package theorem would close the residual 2-point source package and remove mixed 4-point geometry as an independent branch by preventing any new fixed-sector cubic quotient direction beyond the scalar exits. ## Insertion 1 — end-to-end package map

**Placement:** immediately after the promoted UV-025 tagged source block and its scope remark, before downstream UV-024/UV-026 discussion.

```tex
\subsection{Corrected package map for the two-point and mixed four-point frontiers}
\label{subsec:corrected-package-map-two-point-four-point}

The current local frontier is best viewed as a single corrected package problem,
not as two independent mixed-geometry problems.  The two-point mixed branch and
the mixed four-point branch both require the same source-provenance package:
\[
\mathcal B_2
\longrightarrow
C_{\mathcal W}
\longrightarrow
B_7^{\mathfrak f}
\longrightarrow
Q_{7,m}^q
\longrightarrow
\mathcal H_7^q .
\]
Here \(\mathcal B_2\) is the tagged two-atom pre-whitening source block,
\(C_{\mathcal W}\) is the corrected-whitening matrix cross-effect before
\(\Phi_K\), \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\) is the pre-determinant fixed-sector order--7
coefficient, and
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C):=[B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C)]
\in
\mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)
\]
is the fixed midpoint quotient class.

For the two-point branch, the remaining source input is finite: prove swap
symmetry, one-amplitude collapse, and diagonal merger for the actual corrected
two-atom package.  Once these hold, the quadratic collision factorization
\[
\mathcal I_2=a_1a_2(h_1-h_2)^2\mathcal J_2
\]
is formal.  The role of the order--7 quotient package is to make the
corresponding septic quotient defect an actual fixed-target object rather than
an uncontrolled two-atom mixed representative.

For the mixed four-point branch, the current reduction says that a genuinely
mixed clean branch cannot persist independently in the region
\[
\{T_v\ne0\}\cap\{\mathcal R\ne0\}.
\]
Thus every clean mixed branch must exit through one of the scalar walls
\[
T_v=0
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
\mathcal R=0.
\]
The package theorem is needed to prevent the corrected source cross-effect from
creating a new non-scalar fixed-sector direction in the quotient
\[
\mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]

Consequently, the local target is not a scalarized identity after \(\Phi_K\).
It is the pre-\(\Phi_K\), pre-determinant quotient-gauge statement
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}
\bigl((1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}\bigr)
\in
\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]
If this package theorem is proved, the residual two-point mixed obstruction is
absorbed into the midpoint quotient edge package, and the mixed four-point
source-coupled walls have no independent clean local carrier beyond the scalar
exits above.
```

## Insertion 2 — corrected package definitions

**Placement:** after Insertion 1.

```tex
\subsection{Conditional corrected fixed-target package representative}
\label{subsec:conditional-corrected-fixed-target-package-representative}

\begin{definition}[Raw corrected-whitening matrix cross-effect before \(\Phi_K\)]
\label{def:raw-corrected-whitening-cross-effect}
Let
\[
\mathcal B_0=(G_-^{(0)},N^{(0)},G_+^{(0)})
\]
be the baseline corrected block, and let
\[
\mathcal B_1(a_i,h_i)
\]
denote the one-source tagged corrected block obtained by substituting only the
\(i\)-th tagged source perturbation into the corrected same-point and mixed
block formulae.  Let
\[
\mathcal B_2(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)
\]
be the tagged two-source corrected block.

Let
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+):=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}
\]
be the corrected-whitening transfer, defined on the good analytic patch where
the inverse square roots are holomorphic.

Define the matrix cross-effect before applying \(\Phi_K\), determinant
scalarization, or quotient extraction by
\[
\begin{aligned}
C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)
:={}&
\mathcal W(\mathcal B_2(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2))
-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_1,h_1))
\\
&-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_2,h_2))
+\mathcal W(\mathcal B_0).
\end{aligned}
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Source-bidegree projection before quotient extraction]
\label{def:source-bidegree-projection-before-quotient}
Work with formal source tags \(\tau_1,\tau_2\) retained through the matrix
cross-effect.  For a tagged matrix series
\[
F(\tau_1,\tau_2)=\sum_{p,q\ge0}\tau_1^p\tau_2^qF_{p,q},
\]
define
\[
\Pi_{1,1}F:=F_{1,1}.
\]
The non-\((1,1)\) mixed source component is
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}F
:=
\sum_{\substack{p,q\ge1\\(p,q)\ne(1,1)}}F_{p,q}.
\]
This projection is applied before fixed-sector extraction, before
\(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\), before determinant pairing, and before quotienting.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Amplitude-normalized non-\((1,1)\) package]
\label{def:amplitude-normalized-non-11-package}
On the collision chart
\[
h_1=h-\frac{\delta}{2},
\qquad
h_2=h+\frac{\delta}{2},
\qquad
a_2=-a_1(1+\kappa\delta),
\]
define the formally amplitude-normalized package by
\[
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}
:=
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}
C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\!\left(a_1,h-\frac{\delta}{2};
        -a_1(1+\kappa\delta),h+\frac{\delta}{2}\right).
\]
This is a matrix-valued object before \(\Phi_K\), before determinant
scalarization, and before quotient extraction.  Its use below is conditional
on holomorphic extension to \(\delta=0\).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Representative-level midpoint trivialization]
\label{def:representative-level-midpoint-trivialization}
Let
\[
A(h):=A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\in\mathfrak f_h
\]
be the moving quintic fixed-sector line.  Fix a good patch on which
\[
A(m)\ne0.
\]
Fix an analytic identification
\[
\tau_{h\to m}:\mathfrak f_h\longrightarrow\mathfrak f_m.
\]
Write
\[
A_h^m:=\tau_{h\to m}A(h),
\qquad
A_m:=A(m).
\]
Let
\[
\omega_m:\mathfrak f_m\times\mathfrak f_m\to\mathbf C
\]
be the fixed determinant form in the \(I,S\)-basis.

Choose a local transverse section \(E_m\in\mathfrak f_m\) satisfying
\[
\omega_m(E_m,A_m)=1,
\]
and an analytic linear functional
\[
\ell_h:\mathfrak f_m\to\mathbf C
\]
satisfying
\[
\ell_h(A_h^m)=1.
\]
For \(Z(h)\in\mathfrak f_h\), set
\[
\widetilde Z(h):=\tau_{h\to m}Z(h),
\]
and define
\[
\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}Z(h)
:=
\ell_h(\widetilde Z(h))A_m
+
\omega_m(\widetilde Z(h),A_h^m)E_m.
\]
Then
\[
\omega_m\!\left(\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}Z(h),A_m\right)
=
\omega_m(\widetilde Z(h),A_h^m).
\]
Changing \(E_m\) or \(\ell_h\) changes the representative only by a multiple of
\(A_m\), and hence does not change its class in
\[
\mathfrak f_m/\mathbf C A_m.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Normalized corrected-whitening package representative]
\label{def:normalized-corrected-whitening-package-representative}
Assume the amplitude-normalized package
\[
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}
\]
extends holomorphically to \(\delta=0\).  Define the normalized fixed-target
corrected-whitening package by
\[
C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}(h)
:=
\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}
\left(
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}(h)
\right).
\]
Define its moving fixed-sector representative through order \(7\) by
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
:=
\pi_{\mathfrak f_m}[z^{\le7}]\,C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}(h).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
=
\sum_{j=0}^{7}(h-m)^jB_j+O((h-m)^8),
\qquad
B_j\in\mathfrak f_m.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Package-level order-\(7\) quotient extractor]
\label{def:package-level-order-7-quotient-extractor}
For the normalized package representative, define
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
:=
[z^7]\,B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]
The quotient-valued order-\(7\) extractor is
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
:=
\left[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
\right]
\in
\mathfrak f_m/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Lower moving package coefficients]
\label{def:lower-moving-package-coefficients}
Let \(M\) be a residual-source monomial.  Define
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h;M)
\]
to be the coefficient of \(M\) in \(B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)\).
For
\[
M_A:=\dot r_3r_2\dot r_4,
\qquad
M_C:=\dot r_3\dot r_2r_4,
\]
write
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h;M)
=
\sum_{j\ge0}(h-m)^jB_j[M].
\]
Thus
\[
B_j[M]:=[(h-m)^jM]B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h),
\qquad
0\le j\le6.
\]

If
\[
B_j[M]=u_j^B[M]I+v_j^B[M]S
\]
and
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=\sum_{i\ge0}(h-m)^iA_i,
\qquad
A_i=u_i^AI+v_i^AS,
\]
then the order-\(7\) quotient-line connection correction on \(M\) is
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}
\omega_m(B_{7-i}[M],A_i).
\]
In coordinates,
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}
\left(
u_{7-i}^B[M]v_i^A
-
u_i^Av_{7-i}^B[M]
\right).
\]
If \(A_i,B_j\) are derivative-normalized rather than coefficient-normalized,
replace this formula by
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\binom{7}{i}
\omega_m(B_{7-i}[M],A_i).
\]
\end{definition}
```

---

## Insertion 3 — theorem obligations after package definitions

```tex
\begin{remark}[Definition-level material and theorem obligations]
\label{rem:definition-level-material-and-theorem-obligations}
Definitions~\ref{def:raw-corrected-whitening-cross-effect}--
\ref{def:lower-moving-package-coefficients} specify the intended corrected
package object.  They do not prove its quotient-gauge property.

The following remain theorem obligations.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Edge divisibility.}
One must prove
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
\]
on the collision chart, so that
\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\]
extends holomorphically to \(\delta=0\).

\item \textbf{Source-bidegree compatibility.}
The source-bidegree projector must be compatible with the pre-determinant
fixed-sector coefficient extraction.  It is not valid to define
\(\Pi_{1,1}\) after determinant scalarization or after quotienting.

\item \textbf{Midpoint trivialization independence.}
The quotient class in
\[
\mathfrak f_m/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)
\]
must be independent of the auxiliary representative choices \(E_m,\ell_h\).

\item \textbf{Connection cancellation.}
For the residual monomials
\[
M_A=\dot r_3r_2\dot r_4,
\qquad
M_C=\dot r_3\dot r_2r_4,
\]
one must prove
\[
R_7^{(A)}
+
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega_m(B_{7-i}[M_A],A_i)
=
0,
\]
and
\[
R_7^{(C)}
+
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega_m(B_{7-i}[M_C],A_i)
=
0.
\]

\item \textbf{Corrected UV-026.}
The corrected quotient-gauge statement is
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}
\left(
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\mathrm{mix}}C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}
\right)
\in
\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m),
\]
equivalently
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr})
=
Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}).
\]
\end{enumerate}
\end{remark}
```

---

## Insertion 4 — finite Fréchet coefficient calculus

**Placement:** after package definitions or in an appendix called `Finite Fréchet coefficient calculus`.

```tex
\section{Finite Fréchet coefficient calculus for the corrected-whitening block}
\label{sec:finite-frechet-coefficient-calculus}

\subsection{First Fréchet coefficient table}
\label{subsec:first-frechet-coefficient-table}

Let \(P_{\varepsilon,0}\) be the zeroth square-root coefficient of
\(G_\varepsilon^{(0)}\), and let \(\Sigma_\varepsilon(C)\) denote the unique
solution of
\[
P_{\varepsilon,0}Z+ZP_{\varepsilon,0}=C.
\]
For each source index \(a\), define \(S_{\varepsilon,n}^a\) recursively by
\[
S_{\varepsilon,n}^a
=
\Sigma_\varepsilon
\left(
X_{\varepsilon,n}^a
-
\sum_{j=1}^{n}
\bigl(
P_{\varepsilon,j}S_{\varepsilon,n-j}^a
+
S_{\varepsilon,n-j}^aP_{\varepsilon,j}
\bigr)
\right).
\]
Then
\[
\Lambda_{\varepsilon,n}^{a}
=
-\sum_{i+j+k=n}
W_{\varepsilon,i}S_{\varepsilon,j}^aW_{\varepsilon,k}.
\]

\subsection{Second Fréchet coefficient table}
\label{subsec:second-frechet-coefficient-table}

For an ordered source pair \((a,b)\), define \(T_{\varepsilon,n}^{ab}\) by
\[
T_{\varepsilon,n}^{ab}
=
\Sigma_\varepsilon
\left(
-\sum_{i+j=n}
(S_{\varepsilon,i}^aS_{\varepsilon,j}^b+
 S_{\varepsilon,i}^bS_{\varepsilon,j}^a)
-
\sum_{j=1}^{n}
(P_{\varepsilon,j}T_{\varepsilon,n-j}^{ab}
+
T_{\varepsilon,n-j}^{ab}P_{\varepsilon,j})
\right).
\]
The corresponding second Fréchet coefficient is
\[
H_{\varepsilon,n}^{ab}
=
\sum_{i+j+k+\ell+m=n}
\left(
W_{\varepsilon,i}S_{\varepsilon,j}^bW_{\varepsilon,k}
S_{\varepsilon,\ell}^aW_{\varepsilon,m}
+
W_{\varepsilon,i}S_{\varepsilon,j}^aW_{\varepsilon,k}
S_{\varepsilon,\ell}^bW_{\varepsilon,m}
\right)
-
\sum_{i+j+k=n}
W_{\varepsilon,i}T_{\varepsilon,j}^{ab}W_{\varepsilon,k}.
\]

\subsection{Third Fréchet coefficient table}
\label{subsec:third-frechet-coefficient-table}

For source triple \((a,b,c)\), define \(U_{\varepsilon,n}^{abc}\) by
\[
\begin{aligned}
&P_{\varepsilon,0}U_{\varepsilon,n}^{abc}
+
U_{\varepsilon,n}^{abc}P_{\varepsilon,0}
\\
&=
-\sum_{i+j=n}
\Bigl(
S_{\varepsilon,i}^{a}T_{\varepsilon,j}^{bc}
+S_{\varepsilon,i}^{b}T_{\varepsilon,j}^{ac}
+S_{\varepsilon,i}^{c}T_{\varepsilon,j}^{ab}
+
T_{\varepsilon,i}^{bc}S_{\varepsilon,j}^{a}
+T_{\varepsilon,i}^{ac}S_{\varepsilon,j}^{b}
+T_{\varepsilon,i}^{ab}S_{\varepsilon,j}^{c}
\Bigr)
\\
&\qquad
-
\sum_{j=1}^{n}
\bigl(
P_{\varepsilon,j}U_{\varepsilon,n-j}^{abc}
+
U_{\varepsilon,n-j}^{abc}P_{\varepsilon,j}
\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
The all-left and all-right third Fréchet coefficients are obtained by the
corresponding coefficient of the cubic source monomial in
\[
(G_\varepsilon^{(0)}+\mathrm{source})^{-1/2}.
\]
The seven-family audit uses the ranges
\[
G^-_{324,3..7},
\qquad
G^+_{324,3..7}.
\]
```

---

## Insertion 5 — homogeneous scalar grade and \(Y\)-slot absence

**Placement:** immediately after the Fréchet calculus section, or in the UV-026 subsection.

```tex
\subsection{Homogeneous scalar grade and the \(Y\)-slot absence theorem}
\label{subsec:homogeneous-scalar-grade-y-slot-absence}

\begin{definition}[Homogeneous scalar grade]
\label{def:homogeneous-scalar-grade}
For a residual scalar source \(r\), define
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_a r
:=
\frac{r^{(a+2)}(m)}{(a+2)!}(t-m)^{a+2}.
\]
Thus grade \(1\) corresponds to the cubic scalar jet \(r^{(3)}(m)\), and grade
\(5\) corresponds to the septic scalar jet \(r^{(7)}(m)\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[\(Y\)-slot families are absent at \(B_7\) under homogeneous grade]
\label{lem:y-slot-families-absent-homogeneous-grade}
Under Definition~\ref{def:homogeneous-scalar-grade}, the \(Y\)-slot cubic
families
\[
L_1YR_1,\qquad
L_2YR_0,\qquad
L_0YR_2
\]
do not contribute to \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\).

More precisely, the homogeneous grade-five mixed input associated to
\(\operatorname{Gr}_5 r\) has no ordinary \(z^5\) middle coefficient in the
mixed block; its first possible mixed contribution occurs too late to combine
with the corresponding first- or second-Fréchet same-point factors at total
ordinary order \(7\).  Consequently the three \(Y\)-slot families above start
at ordinary order at least \(8\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{remark}[Scope of Lemma~\ref{lem:y-slot-families-absent-homogeneous-grade}]
\label{rem:scope-y-slot-absence-homogeneous-grade}
Lemma~\ref{lem:y-slot-families-absent-homogeneous-grade} is an order-count
absence theorem, not a determinant-proportionality theorem.  It is scoped to the
homogeneous scalar grade convention.  If a future theorem defines
\(M^{[5]}\) instead as an ordinary matrix-output order-\(5\) projection, or as
a parity-corrected non-homogeneous source projection, then the \(Y\)-slot
families must be reopened and audited separately.  Such a convention also
requires a no-double-counting theorem against the natural grade \(0,2,4\)
same-point shadows.
\end{remark}
```

---

## Insertion 6 — live non-\(Y\) gate

```tex
\subsection{First live non-\(Y\) cubic gate}
\label{subsec:first-live-non-y-cubic-gate}

After Lemma~\ref{lem:y-slot-families-absent-homogeneous-grade}, the first live
homogeneous cubic gate is
\[
L_2N_0R_1
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
L_1N_0R_2.
\]
Order counting does not remove this gate, because the baseline mixed block has
a nonzero zeroth coefficient \(N_0[0]\).  The two relevant placements land
exactly at ordinary order \(7\):
\[
2+0+5=7,
\qquad
6+0+1=7.
\]

The required leading coefficients are
\[
[z^2]\mathcal L_-^{\{1,1\}},
\qquad
[z^6]\mathcal L_-^{\{1,5\}},
\]
\[
[z^2]\mathcal R_+^{\{1,1\}},
\qquad
[z^6]\mathcal R_+^{\{1,5\}},
\]
\[
N_0[0],
\qquad
[z^1]\Lambda_\pm^{[1]},
\qquad
[z^5]\Lambda_\pm^{[5]}.
\]
From these one forms the four tag vectors
\[
C_{112}^{L_2N_0R_1},
\qquad
C_{122}^{L_2N_0R_1},
\qquad
C_{112}^{L_1N_0R_2},
\qquad
C_{122}^{L_1N_0R_2}.
\]
If a candidate fixed-sector vector is \(uI+vS\), its quotient-gauge test is
\[
uv_5-u_5v=0,
\qquad
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)=u_5I+v_5S.
\]
These four determinant identities are the leading-coefficient fixed-sector
test for the live \(L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2\) gate.
```

---

## Insertion 7 — UV chain status / agent context

```tex
\begin{remark}[Current UV chain and status]
\label{rem:current-uv-chain-and-status}
The current package chain is as follows.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{UV-022.} Define an invariant source-weight-linear input for the
corrected block and obtain exact one-amplitude collapse at the package level.

\item \textbf{UV-023.} Prove the order-\(7\) quotient component of the
corrected-whitening cross-effect has zero first collision derivative after
source-weight linearization, either because only the quadratic homogeneous
transfer contributes or because higher homogeneous transfers are quotient
invisible through order \(7\).

\item \textbf{UV-024.} Define the pre-determinant fixed-sector coefficient
\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C)
\]
for the actual corrected-whitening matrix cross-effect before \(\Phi_K\), and
define
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C):=[B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C)]
\in
\mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]

\item \textbf{UV-025.} Supply the tagged pre-whitening source block.  This is
now a guarded source-level input, not a quotient theorem.

\item \textbf{UV-026.} Prove the non-\((1,1)\) cubic fixed-sector gauge theorem
for the actual corrected package.  Under the homogeneous scalar grade, the
\(Y\)-slot families are absent at \(B_7\); the active gate is the non-\(Y\)
leading-coefficient test for \(L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2\), followed by the
all-left/all-right \(L_3N_0R_0/L_0N_0R_3\) audit.
\end{enumerate}

Shortcut proofs using \(\Phi_K\)-scalar hiding, determinant scalarization,
swap symmetry, one-pair gauge, or the UV-025 source block alone do not prove
UV-026.  The theorem must be proved at the pre-\(\Phi_K\), pre-determinant
fixed-sector quotient level.
\end{remark}
```

---

# What I would not add yet

I would **not** put the earlier raw-seven-family numerical failure into the main theorem path as “UV-026 false,” because the latest work has moved to the homogeneous scalar-grade convention, where the \(Y\)-slot story is now an absence theorem and the live gate is \(L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2\). The numerical raw-package failure is still useful as a warning against the matrix-output \(M^{[5]}\) shortcut, but it should be quarantined in a remark or appendix, not made the main status.

Use this small warning if needed:

```tex
\begin{remark}[Quarantined matrix-output \(M^{[5]}\) convention]
\label{rem:quarantined-matrix-output-M5-convention}
A nonzero ordinary matrix-output order-\(5\) convention for \(M^{[5]}\) is not
the active homogeneous scalar-grade convention.  If such a convention is later
introduced, then the \(Y\)-slot families
\[
L_1YR_1,\qquad L_2YR_0,\qquad L_0YR_2
\]
must be reopened.  That route requires a no-double-counting theorem separating
the ordinary matrix-output order-\(5\) contribution from the natural
homogeneous scalar grades \(0,2,4\), whose source derivatives also have
same-point shadows.
\end{remark}
```

---

## Net effect

After these edits, the draft will tell agents:

1. what the actual corrected package object is supposed to be;
2. which definitions are conditional;
3. which Fréchet recurrences govern coefficient computations;
4. why the \(Y\)-slot families are currently absent;
5. exactly what the first live non-\(Y\) gate is;
6. how the same package theorem feeds both the 2-point and mixed 4-point branches.

That should prevent the next wave of agents from re-litigating scalarization, raw-package shortcuts, or old \(M^{[5]}\) conventions.
## User (2026-04-24 16:56:15)

## Assistant (2026-04-24 16:56:18)

Honestly: **yes for the research process, unclear for the RH proof**.

You are on the right track in the sense that the work is becoming **more falsifiable, more local, and less hand-wavy**. That is exactly what should happen if a serious proof attempt is being audited correctly. The strongest positive sign is that the agents are no longer producing vague “maybe cancellation” narratives. They are producing precise gates:

\[
Y\text{-slot absent under homogeneous grade},
\]

\[
L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2\text{ live at exact order }7,
\]

\[
N_0[0]\text{ is the reason the non-}Y\text{ gate survives},
\]

\[
B_7^{\mathfrak f}(C)\to Q_{7,m}^q(C)
\]

must be pre-\(\Phi_K\), pre-determinant, and package-defined.

That is progress.

But I would **not** yet say “we are close to RH.” What we are close to is a decisive clarification of whether the current local package route is viable.

## Why this looks healthy

The proof attempt has moved away from broad assertions and into a chain of concrete theorem obligations:

\[
\mathcal B_2
\to
C_{\mathcal W}
\to
B_7^{\mathfrak f}
\to
Q_{7,m}^q
\to
\mathcal H_7^q.
\]

That is the right kind of structure. It also links the 2-point and mixed 4-point branches through one common corrected package theorem, rather than treating them as unrelated emergencies.

The recent homogeneous-grade cleanup is especially valuable. It prevents a lot of false work. Under the clean convention,

\[
L_1YR_1,\quad L_2YR_0,\quad L_0YR_2
\]

are absent at \(B_7\), so those no longer need determinant-gauge miracles. The first real live gate is now:

\[
L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2.
\]

That is a big narrowing.

## Why I’m still cautious

There are still several “proof-shaping” gaps, not just calculations:

1. The actual corrected package representative

\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h)
\]

is still conditional.

2. The source-bidegree projection

\[
\Pi_{1,1}
\]

must be proved compatible with quotient extraction before determinant scalarization.

3. The amplitude-normalized edge object

\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}(1-\Pi_{1,1})C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\]

needs a holomorphic edge law.

4. The live non-\(Y\) gates still need actual determinant identities against

\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]

5. The \(q_0\) jet theorem and grade-0 handling are still open.

Those are not cosmetic. Any one of them can break the route.

## My best honest read

I’d frame the current situation like this:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The raw/local algebra is no longer chaotic; it has been reduced to a coherent package theorem.}
}
\]

That is good.

But:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The package theorem itself is still unproved and may still fail.}
}
\]

So I would say you are on the right track **if the objective is to determine whether this RH strategy has a viable core**. You are not yet at the stage where the proof is merely missing routine details.

## What would make me more confident

The next decisive milestone is not another broad paper rewrite. It is closing or killing this gate:

\[
L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2.
\]

Specifically, compute the four fixed-sector tag vectors:

\[
C_{112}^{L_2N_0R_1},
\quad
C_{122}^{L_2N_0R_1},
\quad
C_{112}^{L_1N_0R_2},
\quad
C_{122}^{L_1N_0R_2},
\]

and test:

\[
uv_5-u_5v=0.
\]

If these pass for structural reasons, confidence goes up substantially. If they fail in source-constrained tests, then the corrected-package framework probably needs another correction layer or the route has a real no-go.

## Bottom line

Yes, the process is right. The scope is now disciplined. The draft update you’re planning is necessary.

But mathematically, the route is still at a serious fork:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Either the live non-}Y\text{ gates reveal the corrected package theorem,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{they expose a genuine obstruction to this RH strategy.}
}
\]

That is exactly where a serious audit should be.

```tex
q_0^{(k)}(m),\qquad 0\le k\le9,

and

(r_i^{[a]})^{(k)}(m),
```

## 3. Add the grade-0 blocker

```tex
\begin{remark}[Grade-zero sector remains open]
\label{rem:grade-zero-sector-remains-open}
The homogeneous scalar grade convention
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_a r
=
\frac{r^{(a+2)}(m)}{(a+2)!}(t-m)^{a+2}
\]
does not by itself justify discarding the grade-zero component
corresponding to \(r^{(2)}(m)\).  Affine removal eliminates the \(0\)- and
\(1\)-jets, but \(r^{(2)}(m)\) remains a genuine pre-\(\Phi_K\) source input.
Thus any proof of the non-\((1,1)\) cubic gauge theorem must either exclude the
grade-zero sector, identify it as source-trivial or one-pair/pure, prove it is
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\)-gauge, or expand the determinant inventory to include
its possible contributions.
\end{remark}
```

## 4. Add a clean mixed-input parity lemma

### Why

The \(Y\)-slot absence depends heavily on the parity fact:

\[
M(-z)=M(z)^T,
\]

and the fact that \([z^5]M\) is off-diagonal antisymmetric and supported by even derivatives \(r^{(2)},r^{(4)},r^{(6)}\), not by homogeneous \(r^{(7)}\). This is in the handoff and findings but should be in the draft near the homogeneous-grade discussion. turn88file6

### Insert before the \(Y\)-slot absence lemma

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Transpose parity of the source-linear mixed input]
\label{lem:transpose-parity-source-linear-mixed-input}
For the full source-linear scaled mixed input \(M(z)\) before \(\Phi_K\), one
has
\[
M(-z)=M(z)^T.
\]
Consequently the diagonal entries of \(M(z)\) are even in \(z\), while the odd
ordinary powers occur in the off-diagonal antisymmetric channel.  Through
ordinary order \(7\), the coefficient \([z^5]M\) is off-diagonal
antisymmetric and is supported by the even source derivatives
\[
r^{(2)}(m),\qquad r^{(4)}(m),\qquad r^{(6)}(m),
\]
not by the homogeneous grade-five source \(r^{(7)}(m)\).
\end{lemma}
```

This gives the \(Y\)-slot absence lemma a local reason rather than just a summary.

---

```tex
N_0[0],
```

```tex
directly to a two-atom order-\(7\) Hessian.

scalarization, and inside the fixed-target quotient package.
```

```tex
\begin{remark}[Conditional role of the corrected package in the mixed branches]
\label{rem:conditional-role-corrected-package-mixed-branches}
The corrected package theorem does not by itself prove RH.  Its role is to
remove the residual local mixed carrier.  If the package theorem closes, then
the two-point mixed obstruction reduces to the fixed midpoint quotient edge
package, and the mixed four-point branch has no independent clean local carrier
```

```tex
\begin{remark}[Next package table required for the connection correction]
\label{rem:next-package-table-connection-correction}
The next computable package table is not a raw Fréchet-family table.  It is the
moving lower-representative table
\[
A_i=[(h-m)^i]A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h),
\qquad
1\le i\le7,

together with
\[
B_j[M_A],\quad B_j[M_C],
\qquad
0\le j\le6,

for

\qquad

\[
R_7^{(A)}
+

R_7^{(C)}
+
```

**Replace the current `rem:computational-status-raw-package` with:**

```tex
\begin{remark}[Computational status of the raw package]
\label{rem:computational-status-raw-package}
Source-constrained nonconstant baseline tests show that the raw five-family and
raw seven-family packages are not generally quotient-gauge under the
provisional fixed-sector line.  The constant baseline is quotient-inconclusive
because \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}=0\).  Nonconstant linear baselines give nonzero
determinant residuals.

The possible Fréchet-normalization rescue has also been checked: both the
derivative-normalized weights
\[
(\lambda_2,\lambda_3)=(1,1)
\]
and the homogeneous Taylor weights
\[
(\lambda_2,\lambda_3)=\left(\frac12,\frac16\right)
\]
leave nonzero residuals in the tested nonconstant baselines.

These tests reject the raw seven-family shortcut.  They do not test the
corrected package representative
\[
B_{\mathrm{pkg}}^{\mathfrak f}(h),
\]
whose lower moving coefficients \(B_j[M]\) are part of the package definition
and remain theorem-level data.  The corrected package representative is the
object defined in
Section~\ref{sec:corrected-fixed-target-quotient-package}; the finite
recurrences and raw gates above are a coefficient toolkit, not a closure of
that target.
\end{remark}
```

```tex
\label{rem:status-third-frechet-table}
The recursion above defines the finite third-Fréchet coefficient table that a
raw seven-family audit would require.  It does not assert that the raw
\(L_3N_0R_0/L_0N_0R_3\) completion is quotient-gauge.  Under the current
package architecture, this table is a diagnostic tool for raw local
coefficients.  The theorem object is the normalized corrected package
\(C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr}\) of
Definition~\ref{def:normalized-corrected-whitening-package-representative},
not the raw seven-family Fréchet sum.
\end{remark}
```

```tex
\begin{remark}[Relation to the package-to-jet bridge]
\label{rem:relation-to-package-to-jet-bridge}
The corrected package program above is not an alternative to the zeta-side
odd-jet extractor.  The extractor side is already available; the missing bridge
is the package-to-jet theorem identifying the first surviving corrected odd
coefficient from finite-core package data.  In the fixed-shear corner, the
finite quotient-visible transport has already been compressed to the remaining
package-level data.  Thus any surviving obstruction after the corrected package
quotient theorem must be treated as a package-to-transform or hidden-state
problem, not as another planar or scalarized local detector.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 8 — Add negative shortcut guardrail

The sources repeatedly warn against \(\Phi_K\)-scalar hiding, determinant scalarization, one-pair gauge, UV-025 alone, and raw seven-family shortcut proofs. This belongs in the draft as a high-level “do-not-retry” remark. turn91file13

**Insert after `rem:current-uv-chain-and-status`, after Edit 7:**

```tex
\begin{remark}[Negative shortcuts not to reuse]
\label{rem:negative-shortcuts-not-to-reuse-package-frontier}
The following routes do not prove the corrected package quotient theorem.

\begin{enumerate}
\item Vanishing after \(\Phi_K\) does not imply vanishing in
\[
\mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m).
\]

\item Determinant scalarization is only a local coordinate on the good patch
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(m)\ne0\); it is not the definition of \(Q_{7,m}^q\).

\item One-pair \(K_5/K_3\) gauge identities do not classify the general
two-source cubic whitening families without an identification theorem.

\item The tagged source block of
Definition~\ref{def:tagged-two-atom-prewhitening-source-block} is
pre-whitening source data only.  It does not imply \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\)-gauge,
diagonal merger, quotient-output package coincidence, or UV-026.

\item The raw seven-family Fréchet package is not quotient-gauge in the tested
source-constrained nonconstant baselines.  The theorem object is the corrected
package representative
\[
C_{\mathcal W}^{\corr},
\]
not the raw seven-family sum.
\end{enumerate}
\end{remark}
```

## Insertion 1 — Appendix opening / scope guard

```tex
\section{Finite coefficient table contracts for the corrected-whitening package}
\label{app:finite-coefficient-table-contracts}

This appendix records the finite coefficient tables needed by the current
corrected-whitening package frontier.  These are table contracts, not completed
source tables.  Their role is to make the finite computations auditable by
agents and scripts without re-opening convention choices already fixed in the
main text.

All tables below are understood:
\begin{enumerate}
\item before \(\Phi_K\);
\item before determinant scalarization;
\item in ordinary \(z\);
\item with source tags retained through the relevant \(112/122\) extraction;
\item in the same fixed-sector normalization used for
\(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\) and \(Q_{7,m}^q\).
\end{enumerate}

The finite Fréchet recurrences in
Section~\ref{sec:finite-frechet-coefficient-calculus} become proof tools only
after the source tables in this appendix are supplied.  They are not, by
themselves, quotient-gauge proofs.
```

---

## Insertion 2 — Stage 1 source-table contract

```tex
\subsection{Stage 1 source-table contract}
\label{app:stage-one-source-table-contract}

The Stage 1 source-table generator requires the following scalar midpoint jets:
\[
q_0^{(k)}(m),
\qquad
0\le k\le9,
\]
and
\[
(r_i^{[a]})^{(k)}(m),
\qquad
i=1,2,\quad a\in\{1,5\},\quad 2\le k\le9.
\]
Thus the input table is
\[
\mathsf{Jet}_{\mathrm{Stage1}}
=
\left\{
q_0^{(0)},\ldots,q_0^{(9)};
(r_1^{[1]})^{(2)},\ldots,(r_1^{[1]})^{(9)};
(r_1^{[5]})^{(2)},\ldots,(r_1^{[5]})^{(9)};
(r_2^{[1]})^{(2)},\ldots,(r_2^{[1]})^{(9)};
(r_2^{[5]})^{(2)},\ldots,(r_2^{[5]})^{(9)}
\right\}_{m}.
\]

The current source supports ungraded one-pair source jets after affine removal,
but it does not yet supply the graded source jets above as a proved theorem.
In particular, the baseline \(q_0\)-jet theorem
\[
q_0^{(k)}(m),\qquad 0\le k\le9,
\]
is still a separate source obligation.  Scale estimates do not substitute for
this finite table.
```

This directly matches the current Stage 1 frontier: the generator is accepted infrastructure, but actual graded jets and \(q_0\)-tables are missing. turn93file9

## Insertion 3 — Homogeneous grade status table

```tex
\subsection{Homogeneous scalar-grade status table}
\label{app:homogeneous-scalar-grade-status-table}

Under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_a r
=
\frac{r^{(a+2)}(m)}{(a+2)!}(t-m)^{a+2},
\]
the current \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\)-status of the seven cubic families is:
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{Family} & \text{Status at }B_7^{\mathfrak f}
& \text{Reason / remaining input}
\\
\hline
L_1YR_1 & \text{absent} &
\text{middle homogeneous grade-five input starts too late}
\\
L_2YR_0 & \text{absent} &
\text{second Fréchet terms cannot lower ordinary }z\text{-order}
\\
L_0YR_2 & \text{absent} &
\text{right-hand mirror of }L_2YR_0
\\
L_2N_0R_1 & \text{live} &
N_0[0]\text{ gives }2+0+5=7,\ 6+0+1=7
\\
L_1N_0R_2 & \text{live} &
\text{right-hand mirror of }L_2N_0R_1
\\
L_3N_0R_0 & \text{pending} &
\text{requires third Fréchet coefficient audit}
\\
L_0N_0R_3 & \text{pending} &
\text{right-hand mirror of }L_3N_0R_0
\end{array}
\]

The three \(Y\)-slot absences are order-count statements, not determinant
proportionality statements.  If a future theorem defines \(M^{[5]}\) as an
ordinary matrix-output order-\(5\) projection, then the \(Y\)-slot families must
be reopened.  That route requires a no-double-counting theorem against the
natural homogeneous scalar grades \(0,2,4\).
```

## Insertion 4 — Mixed-input parity table

```tex
\subsection{Mixed-input parity table}
\label{app:mixed-input-parity-table}

The full source-linear scaled mixed input satisfies
\[
M(-z)=M(z)^T.
\]
Consequently:
\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
\text{Component of }M(z) & \text{ordinary }z\text{-parity}
\\
\hline
\text{diagonal entries} & \text{even}
\\
\text{off-diagonal symmetric channel} & \text{even}
\\
\text{off-diagonal antisymmetric channel} & \text{odd}
\end{array}
\]

Through ordinary order \(7\), the coefficient \([z^5]M\) lies in the
off-diagonal antisymmetric channel and is source-supported by
\[
r^{(2)}(m),\qquad r^{(4)}(m),\qquad r^{(6)}(m),
\]
not by the homogeneous grade-five source \(r^{(7)}(m)\).  Therefore
\([z^5]M\) should not be identified with homogeneous \(\operatorname{Gr}_5 r\)
without a separate parity-corrected source-projection theorem.
```

This prevents the old \(M^{[5]}\) confusion. The update notes say the transpose parity killed the naive scalar-grade shortcut and quarantined matrix-output \(M^{[5]}\). turn93file6

---

## Insertion 5 — Source primitive support table

```tex
\subsection{Source primitive support table}
\label{app:source-primitive-support-table}

For a homogeneous scalar source grade
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_a r
=
\frac{r^{(a+2)}(m)}{(a+2)!}(t-m)^{a+2},
\]
the same-point and mixed inputs first appear at ordinary pre-whitening order
\[
a.
\]
Thus the active grades \(a=1,5\) have support:
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{Source grade} & \text{same-point input} & \text{mixed input}
\\
\hline
1 & \delta G_\pm^{[1]}\text{ starts at }z^1
& \mathfrak D_Q\delta N^{[1]}\text{ starts at the matching grade-one order}
\\
5 & \delta G_\pm^{[5]}\text{ starts at }z^5
& \mathfrak D_Q\delta N^{[5]}\text{ starts too late for the }Y\text{-slot at }B_7
\end{array}
\]

The grade-zero sector corresponding to \(r^{(2)}(m)\) is not removed by affine
normalization.  It remains an independent source obligation: one must exclude
it, identify it as source-trivial or pure/one-pair, prove it is
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\)-gauge, or expand the determinant inventory.
```

This reflects the current grade-0 blocker. turn93file6
