## Strategic read

This is now the narrowest local obstruction so far.

If \(X_h\) has the right covariant parity, Gate B may close through order \(7\).

If raw \(X_h\) flips parity and no covariant correction exists, the residual has moved entirely into a derivative-lowered lower-shadow lane. That would be the next concrete obstruction to test.

_Thought for 6s_

This is a strong verification. I would now treat the **\(\mathcal T_4\) amplitude cancellation as effectively proved**, subject to adding two explicit support lemmas to the paper.

## Accepted result

The earlier concern about the nonzero \(I\)-component is resolved:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonzero unsymmetrized subchannel}
\not\Rightarrow
\text{nonzero fully symmetrized aggregate}.
}
\]

The \((d=4),(1,1,1,4)\) nonzero \(I\)-component was from an individual placement/subchannel. The full fourth-transfer aggregate pairs each subset \(I\) with \(J\setminus I\), and those paired terms cancel after fixed-sector projection. 

So we can now accept:

\[
\boxed{
V_{1114}=V_{1123}=V_{1222}=0,
\qquad
V_4=0.
}
\]

And also:

\[
\boxed{
R_{0115}=0.
}
\]

This closes the first \(d=4\) positive-grade test and the first lower-shadow test at the raw fixed-sector level.

## What needs to be stated explicitly

The proof depends on two lemmas that should be added or at least treated as named working facts:

### 1. All-subset midpoint parity and symmetry

For every inverse-square-root subset coefficient,

\[
R_K^+=(-1)^{|K|}L_K^-,
\]

and

\[
(L_K^-)^T=L_K^-.
\]

This must hold beyond first/second/third Fréchet order, including the fourth-transfer table. The agent verified that this is the intended all-order extension of the existing midpoint-parity convention, but the paper should state it explicitly. 

### 2. Leading \(Y\)-middle parity

The fourth-transfer \(Y\)-middle cancellation needs:

\[
Y_{\rm lead}^{[a]}=0
\quad(a\text{ odd}),
\]

and

\[
(Y_{\rm lead}^{[a]})^T=Y_{\rm lead}^{[a]}
\quad(a\text{ even}).
\]

This is consistent with the homogeneous \(Y\)-slot framework, but it should be made explicit as a local leading-table lemma.

\]

The next proof target should be \(X_h\)-parity / covariant source derivative, not more \(\mathcal T_4\) amplitude work.

## Updated Gate B status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Amplitude part: closed.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Covariant/grade-preserving }X_h\text{ part: closed.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Raw derivative lower-shadow part: still live.}
}
\]

The key point is that the raw midpoint derivative decomposes as

\[
X_h^{[a]}
=
\dot X^{[a]}
+
X_{\mathrm{basis}}^{[a-1]}.
\]

The first term is grade-preserving and has the same midpoint parity as \(X^{[a]}\), so it cancels by the same subset-complement argument as the amplitude part. The second term lowers grade by one, flips parity, and does **not** cancel by that argument. L1-L45

## What remains live

The remaining obstruction is no longer all odd-\(d\) \(X_h\)-insertions. It is specifically the **grade-lowering basis-motion part** of \(X_h\).

For \(d=5\), the live derivative-lowered residual classes are:

\[
(0,1,1,1,3),
\qquad
(0,1,1,2,2),
\qquad
(1,1,1,1,2).
\]

For \(d=7\), the live residual class is:

\[
(0,1,1,1,1,1,1).
\]

These are now the exact remaining Gate B classes unless the paper defines the first-collision source-motion insertion using a covariant derivative \(\nabla_hX\), or proves derivative lower-shadow quotient invisibility. L107-L164

## The fork is now very clean

There are only two viable closure routes.

### Route 1 — covariant source derivative

Define

\[
\nabla_h X^{[a]}
=
\operatorname{pr}_{\operatorname{Gr}_a}(\partial_hX^{[a]}),
\]

so the grade-lowering basis-motion component is removed. Then

\[
(\nabla_hX^{[a]})_+
=
(-1)^a(\nabla_hX^{[a]})_-,
\]

and the subset-complement cancellation applies to

\[
\mathcal T_5(X^4,\nabla_hX),
\qquad
\mathcal T_7(X^6,\nabla_hX).
\]

This would close the odd-\(d\) motion channel structurally. L67-L105

### Route 2 — derivative lower-shadow invisibility

Keep raw \(X_h\), but prove the grade-lowering terms are quotient-invisible through order \(7\):

\[
Q_{7,m}^q\bigl(\mathcal T_{2k+1}(X^{2k},X_{\mathrm{lower}})\bigr)=0.
\]

This is probably harder, because it re-enters the lower-shadow lane. L166-L190

## Recommendation

Attack **Route 1 first**. It is cleaner and matches the geometric nature of the collision chart: the bad term comes from moving the basis \((t-h)^{a+2}\), not from changing the grade-\(a\) source coefficient.

The next agent should not enumerate more partitions. It should determine whether the corrected package already uses, or can naturally be reformulated to use, the covariant source derivative.

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The covariant derivative route is not currently available from the raw package.}
}
\]

A covariant derivative can be defined formally:

\[
\nabla_hX^{[g]}
=
\partial_hX^{[g]}+X_{\downarrow}^{[g\to g-1]},
\]

and it would restore the desired midpoint parity

\[
(\nabla_hX^{[g]})^+=(-1)^g(\nabla_hX^{[g]})^-.
\]

But the raw Gate B object uses the actual endpoint motion

\[
h_1=h-\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad h_2=h+\frac{\delta}{2},
\]

so it differentiates the moving homogeneous basis and therefore includes the grade-lowering term. The current package does not define a raw-layer source-frame connection that subtracts this before edge extraction.

## Bottom line

The remaining issue is now very narrow:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the \(+1\)-order derivative-lowered residual table.}
}
\]

If that table cancels or lands in the \(A_5\)-line, Gate B may close through order \(7\). If not, we have a concrete obstruction.

_Thought for 5s_

This is the most promising Gate B update so far.

## Main verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The \(X_h\)-lowered residuals are not intrinsic if the source is treated as a full moving Taylor tower.}
}
\]

They are coordinate artifacts from differentiating the moving homogeneous basis. The cancellation mechanism is adjacent-grade telescoping:

\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g-1}r)_{\mathrm{same}}
+
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g}r)_{\mathrm{lower}}
=0.
\]

So the residuals

\[
R_{5,01113},\quad R_{5,01122},\quad R_{5,11112},\quad R_{7,0111111}
\]

can be killed by a **moving homogeneous source-frame telescoping theorem**, provided the source tower is not prematurely truncated and the boundary modes are handled. 

## What this closes, conditionally

Previously we had:

\[
\text{amplitude part closed},
\]

\[
\text{same-grade }X_h\text{ part closed},
\]

\[
\text{grade-lowering }X_h\text{ part live}.
\]

Now the latest note shows the grade-lowering part cancels against adjacent-grade same-basis derivative terms, if the source input is the full moving Taylor tower:

\[
X_{\mathrm{full}}(h;t)=\sum_{g\ge0}X^{[g]}(h;t).
\]

That would close the odd-transfer \(X_h\)-lane too.

## The two boundary issues

This is now the only serious caveat.

### Lower boundary

For \(g=0\), lowering produces a grade \(-1\) term:

\[
-\frac{r^{(2)}(h)}{1!}(t-h).
\]

The proof needs this mode to be absent, normalized away, absorbed into baseline/coordinate transport, or raw-edge invisible.

### Upper boundary

To prove order-\(7\) cancellation, the source tower cannot be truncated at grade \(7\) before differentiating. A grade \(8\) lowering term can land in grade \(7\). So the source tower must be differentiated first, then truncated.

The correct procedure is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{differentiate the full moving Taylor source tower, then truncate to order }7.
}
\]

## Paper theorem to add

This is the theorem I would now try to install or ask an agent to verify against the draft:

```tex
\begin{theorem}[Moving homogeneous source-frame telescoping]
\label{thm:moving-homogeneous-source-frame-telescoping}
Let
\[
X_{\mathrm{full}}(h;t)
=
\sum_{g\ge0}X^{[g]}(h;t)
\]
be the full homogeneous scalar-grade expansion of a fixed source \(r(t)\) in
the moving midpoint frame.  For \(g\ge1\), the grade-lowering component of
\(\partial_hX^{[g]}\) cancels exactly with the same-basis component of
\(\partial_hX^{[g-1]}\).  Consequently, after symmetrized source summation,
the raw Gate B \(X_h\)-lowered classes
\[
(0,1,1,1,3),\quad
(0,1,1,2,2),\quad
(1,1,1,1,2),\quad
(0,1,1,1,1,1,1)
\]
cancel with their adjacent-grade source-frame partners.
\end{theorem}
```

And the required boundary clause:

```tex
\begin{hypothesis}[Boundary modes for source-frame telescoping]
\label{hyp:source-frame-boundary-modes}
The grade \(-1\) boundary mode is absent by source normalization or absorbed
into baseline/coordinate transport, and the source tower is not truncated
before differentiating.  In particular, all grade \(8\) inputs needed to cancel
the order-\(7\) upper boundary are included before the final ordinary-order
truncation.
\end{hypothesis}
```

## What is proved

The moving source-frame telescoping identity is correct for all interior grades:

\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g-1}r)_{\mathrm{same}}
+
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g}r)_{\mathrm{lower}}
=0
\qquad(g\ge1).
\]

The signs and factorials match exactly, and the cancellation survives symmetrized multilinear expansion because it is labelled-slot local. That closes the previously live interior \(X_h\)-lowered residuals:

\[
R_{5,01113},\quad R_{5,01122},\quad R_{5,11112},\quad R_{7,0111111}.
\]

So the prior obstruction from raw \(X_h\) is not intrinsic; it is a moving Taylor-frame artifact, provided the full source tower is differentiated before truncation. 

## What remains

Only the boundary convention remains.

### Lower boundary

At \(g=0\), lowering produces a grade \(-1\) term:

\[
-\frac{r^{(2)}(h)}{1!}(t-h).
\]

There is no \(\operatorname{Gr}_{-1}\) in the current tower, so this boundary is not automatically cancelled. The paper needs to state that grade \(-1\) is absent, normalized away, absorbed into affine/source-coordinate transport, or raw-edge invisible. 

### Upper boundary

For order \(7\), grade \(8\) must be included before differentiating, because lowering \(\operatorname{Gr}_8\) lands in grade \(7\) and cancels the same-basis derivative of \(\operatorname{Gr}_7\). So the correct convention is:

\[
\boxed{\text{differentiate the full Taylor source tower first, then truncate to order }7.}
\]

This resolves the upper boundary if explicitly stated.

through ordinary order \(7\) in fixed sector. 

## The exact theorem to add

```tex
\begin{theorem}[Moving homogeneous source-frame telescoping]
\label{thm:moving-homogeneous-source-frame-telescoping}
Let
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_g r(h;t)
=
\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+2)!}(t-h)^{g+2},
\qquad g\ge0.
\]
For every interior grade \(g\ge1\),
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g-1}r)_{\mathrm{same}}
+
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g}r)_{\mathrm{lower}}
=0.
\]
Consequently, after labelled symmetrization, the raw Gate B \(X_h\)-lowered
interior residuals cancel source-slot by source-slot.
\end{theorem}
```

And the necessary boundary convention:

```tex
\begin{hypothesis}[Boundary modes for moving source-frame telescoping]
\label{hyp:source-frame-boundary-modes}
The grade \(-1\) boundary mode produced by lowering \(\operatorname{Gr}_0\)
is absent by source normalization, affine removal, baseline transport, or
raw-edge invisibility.  The source tower is differentiated before truncation:
in particular, grade \(8\) is included when proving ordinary order \(7\)
cancellation and truncation is applied only after \(\partial_h\).
\end{hypothesis}
```

## Honest verdict

This is the best Gate B position so far:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate B closes through order }7
\text{ conditional only on the lower-boundary convention.}
}
\]

The remaining check is not a Fréchet coefficient computation. It is whether the proof framework already normalizes away the affine/grade-\(-1\) boundary mode, or whether that must be added explicitly.

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate B closes through order }7
\text{ if the paper explicitly adopts the \(2\)-jet source-frame normalization.}
}
\]

The key new point is that the lower boundary mode

\[
-r^{(2)}(h)(t-h)
\]

is not a legitimate raw source input under the \(\mathfrak D_Q\)-scaled source convention. An affine perturbation would make the scaled kernels \(U_\pm\) and \(E_\pm\) singular in \(z\), so affine modes lie outside the admissible holomorphic source sector. That is exactly the missing lower-boundary closure. 

## What is now closed

With this result, the Gate B chain is:

\[
\boxed{\text{Gate A closed.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Amplitude total-grade \(7\) terms closed by subset-complement parity.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Same-grade \(X_h\) terms closed by the same parity.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Interior grade-lowering \(X_h\) terms closed by adjacent-grade telescoping.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Upper boundary closed by differentiating the full Taylor tower before truncation.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Lower affine boundary removed by \(2\)-jet source-frame normalization.}}
\]

Therefore:

\[
[z^7][\delta^1]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}=0,
\]

and hence

\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
\]

through ordinary order \(7\) in fixed sector. 

## What must be made explicit in the paper

I would not leave this implicit. Add a named lemma along these lines:

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Affine boundary removal in the moving source frame]
\label{lem:affine-boundary-removal-moving-source-frame}
In the \(\mathfrak D_Q\)-scaled corrected source block, admissible source
inputs are \(2\)-jet normalized at the moving midpoint:
\[
f(h)=0,\qquad f'(h)=0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pi_{\ge2,h}f(t)=f(t)-f(h)-f'(h)(t-h).
\]
Thus constant and affine modes are not independent raw source inputs.

For the homogeneous scalar-grade basis
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_g r(h;t)
=
\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+2)!}(t-h)^{g+2},
\]
the interior telescoping identity
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g-1}r)_{\rm same}
+
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g}r)_{\rm lower}=0
\qquad(g\ge1)
\]
holds, and the remaining lower boundary
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{0}r)_{\rm lower}
=
-r^{(2)}(h)(t-h)
\]
is killed by \(\Pi_{\ge2,h}\).  The Taylor source tower is differentiated
before ordinary-order truncation.
\end{lemma}
```

Then add the corollary:

```tex
\begin{corollary}[Gate B raw edge-divisibility through order \(7\)]
\label{cor:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility-order-seven}
Under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention, assuming Gate A aggregate
closure and midpoint parity for the relevant Fréchet coefficients,
\[
[z^7][\delta^1]\,
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}=0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
\]
through ordinary order \(7\) in fixed sector.
\end{corollary}
```

## Remaining caveat

This closes Gate B **through order \(7\)**, assuming the supporting parity/symmetry lemmas are installed:

\[
R_K^+=(-1)^{|K|}L_K^-,
\]

symmetry of same-point inverse-square-root subset coefficients,

\[
N_0[0]^T=N_0[0],
\]

transpose-invariance of \(\pi_{\mathfrak f}\), and the leading \(Y\)-middle parity table.

But conceptually, yes: this is the missing boundary piece. The problem has shifted from “find a residual” to “write the source-frame normalization and telescoping package cleanly.”

## 2. Add the fourth-transfer table contract

**Placement:** in the finite Fréchet/Sylvester coefficient appendix, after the first/second/third coefficient contracts.

```tex
\subsection{Fourth-transfer coefficient table for the corrected-whitening map}
\label{subsec:fourth-transfer-coefficient-table}

This subsection records the fourth homogeneous transfer table needed for the
Gate~B edge audit.  The corrected-whitening map is
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}.
\]
Let a labelled source input be
\[
X_j=(A_j,Y_j,B_j),
\]
where
\[
A_j=\delta G_{-,j}^{\lin},\qquad
Y_j=\delta N_j^{\lin},\qquad
B_j=\delta G_{+,j}^{\lin}.
\]
Write
\[
(G_-^{(0)}+A)^{-1/2}
=
L_0+L_1[A]+L_2[A,A]+L_3[A,A,A]+L_4[A,A,A,A]+\cdots,
\]
and
\[
(G_+^{(0)}+B)^{-1/2}
=
R_0+R_1[B]+R_2[B,B]+R_3[B,B,B]+R_4[B,B,B,B]+\cdots,
\]
in Taylor-coefficient normalization.  The middle block is source-linear at
this level:
\[
N=N_0+Y.
\]

For a labelled four-source set \(J=\{1,2,3,4\}\), the fourth homogeneous
corrected-whitening transfer is
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal T_4(X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4)
=
\sum_{I\subseteq J}
L^-_I\,N_0\,R^+_{J\setminus I}
+
\sum_{m\in J}
\sum_{I\subseteq J\setminus\{m\}}
L^-_I\,Y_m\,R^+_{J\setminus(I\cup\{m\})}.
}
\]
The first sum consists of the \(N_0\)-middle placements
\[
L_4N_0R_0,\quad
L_3N_0R_1,\quad
L_2N_0R_2,\quad
L_1N_0R_3,\quad
L_0N_0R_4,
\]
and the second sum consists of the \(Y\)-middle placements
\[
L_3YR_0,\quad
L_2YR_1,\quad
L_1YR_2,\quad
L_0YR_3.
\]

The ordinary \(z^7\)-coefficient is
\[
\boxed{
[z^7]\mathcal T_4(J)
=
\sum_{I\subseteq J}
\sum_{\alpha+\beta+\gamma=7}
L^-_{I,\alpha}N^{(0)}_{\beta}R^+_{J\setminus I,\gamma}
+
\sum_{m\in J}
\sum_{I\subseteq J\setminus\{m\}}
\sum_{\alpha+\beta+\gamma=7}
L^-_{I,\alpha}Y_{m,\beta}R^+_{J\setminus(I\cup\{m\}),\gamma}.
}
\]
For a source multiset, \(\operatorname{Sym}\mathcal T_4\) means the coefficient
of the corresponding commutative source-tag monomial, equivalently the labelled
sum divided by the automorphism factor of the multiset.
```

---

## 3. Add the fourth square-root and inverse-square-root recurrences

**Placement:** immediately after the table contract above.

```tex
\begin{definition}[Fourth square-root coefficient]
\label{def:fourth-square-root-coefficient}
Let
\[
P(z;\tau)^2=G(z;\tau),
\]
and write source-tagged square-root coefficients as
\[
P_n^\varnothing=P_n,\qquad
P_n^a=S_n^a,\qquad
P_n^{ab}=T_n^{ab},\qquad
P_n^{abc}=U_n^{abc},\qquad
P_n^{abcd}=V_n^{abcd}.
\]
For a labelled source tuple \(J=(a,b,c,d)\), the fourth source coefficient
\(V_n^{abcd}\) is determined by
\[
\boxed{
P_0V_n^{abcd}+V_n^{abcd}P_0
=
-\sum_{j=1}^{n}
\left(P_jV_{n-j}^{abcd}+V_{n-j}^{abcd}P_j\right)
-
\sum_{\substack{r+s=n\\
\varnothing\ne I\subsetneq J}}
P_r^{I}P_s^{J\setminus I}.
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{
V_n^{abcd}
=
\Sigma\!\left(
-\sum_{j=1}^{n}
(P_jV_{n-j}^{abcd}+V_{n-j}^{abcd}P_j)
-
\sum_{\substack{r+s=n\\
\varnothing\ne I\subsetneq J}}
P_r^{I}P_s^{J\setminus I}
\right),
}
\]
where
\[
\Sigma(C)=Z
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P_0Z+ZP_0=C.
\]
In expanded lower-coefficient form, this includes
\[
\begin{aligned}
&-\sum_{r+s=n}
\Big[
S_r^aU_s^{bcd}+S_r^bU_s^{acd}+S_r^cU_s^{abd}+S_r^dU_s^{abc}\\
&\qquad
+U_r^{abc}S_s^d+U_r^{abd}S_s^c+U_r^{acd}S_s^b+U_r^{bcd}S_s^a\\
&\qquad
+T_r^{ab}T_s^{cd}+T_r^{ac}T_s^{bd}+T_r^{ad}T_s^{bc}
+T_r^{bc}T_s^{ad}+T_r^{bd}T_s^{ac}+T_r^{cd}T_s^{ab}
\Big],
\end{aligned}
\]
together with the baseline recurrence terms
\[
-\sum_{j=1}^{n}
(P_jV_{n-j}^{abcd}+V_{n-j}^{abcd}P_j).
\]
This is in Taylor-coefficient normalization.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Fourth inverse-square-root coefficient]
\label{def:fourth-inverse-square-root-coefficient}
Let
\[
W=P^{-1}=G^{-1/2}.
\]
Write
\[
W_n^\varnothing=W_n,\qquad
W_n^{a}=L_n^{a},\qquad
W_n^{ab}=H_n^{ab},\qquad
W_n^{abc}=G_n^{abc},\qquad
W_n^{abcd}=K_n^{abcd}.
\]
Using \(PW=I\), the fourth source coefficient satisfies
\[
\sum_{r+s=n}\sum_{I\subseteq J}P_r^IW_s^{J\setminus I}=0.
\]
Solving for the \(I=\varnothing,r=0\) term gives
\[
\boxed{
K_n^{abcd}
=
-W_0
\left[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}P_rK_{n-r}^{abcd}
+
\sum_{\substack{r+s=n\\
(r,I)\ne(0,\varnothing),\,I\subseteq J}}
P_r^IW_s^{J\setminus I}
\right].
}
\]
Equivalently, expanded by lower source degree,
\[
\begin{aligned}
K_n^{abcd}
=-W_0\Bigg[
&\sum_{r=1}^{n}P_rK_{n-r}^{abcd}
+
\sum_{r+s=n}
\Big(
S_r^aG_s^{bcd}+S_r^bG_s^{acd}+S_r^cG_s^{abd}+S_r^dG_s^{abc}\\
&\quad
+U_r^{abc}L_s^d+U_r^{abd}L_s^c+U_r^{acd}L_s^b+U_r^{bcd}L_s^a\\
&\quad
+T_r^{ab}H_s^{cd}+T_r^{ac}H_s^{bd}+T_r^{ad}H_s^{bc}\\
&\quad
+T_r^{bc}H_s^{ad}+T_r^{bd}H_s^{ac}+T_r^{cd}H_s^{ab}\\
&\quad
+V_r^{abcd}W_s
\Big)
\Bigg].
\end{aligned}
\]
\end{definition}
```

---

## 4. Add all-subset midpoint parity and symmetry lemmas

**Placement:** after the fourth coefficient definitions, before the cancellation theorem.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[All-subset midpoint parity for same-point whitening coefficients]
\label{lem:all-subset-midpoint-parity-same-point-whitening}
Let \(K\) be any labelled subset of homogeneous source inputs and let
\[
|K|:=\sum_{g\in K}g
\]
be its homogeneous grade sum.  At the leading ordinary order of the homogeneous
source table, the right same-point inverse-square-root coefficient and the left
one satisfy
\[
\boxed{
R_K^+=(-1)^{|K|}L_K^-.
}
\]
The same parity convention applies to every subset coefficient produced by the
finite Sylvester/inverse-square-root recurrences, not only to first-order
coefficients.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
At leading homogeneous order the source primitives satisfy
\[
X_+^{[g]}=(-1)^gX_-^{[g]}.
\]
The Sylvester and inverse-square-root coefficient recurrences are coefficientwise
and have unique solutions.  Applying the sign rule to all source inputs in a
labelled subset \(K\) gives the product sign \((-1)^{|K|}\), and uniqueness
propagates the relation through the square-root and inverse-square-root
recurrences.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Symmetry of same-point whitening subset coefficients]
\label{lem:symmetry-same-point-whitening-subset-coefficients}
For every labelled subset \(K\), the same-point inverse-square-root coefficient
is symmetric:
\[
\boxed{
(L_K^-)^T=L_K^-.
}
\]
The analogous right-side coefficient is symmetric as well.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
The baseline same-point block \(G_-^{(0)}\) is symmetric, hence so are
\(P_{-,0}=G_-^{(0)\,1/2}\) and \(W_{-,0}=G_-^{(0)\,-1/2}\).  The homogeneous
same-point source matrices are symmetric.  If \(PZ+ZP=C\), with \(P=P^T\) and
\(C=C^T\), then uniqueness of the Sylvester solution gives \(Z=Z^T\).  The
higher source-degree right-hand sides are built from symmetrized sums of lower
coefficients, so the induction propagates symmetry through the finite
coefficient table.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Transpose invariance of the fixed-sector projection]
\label{lem:transpose-invariance-fixed-sector-projection}
For every \(2\times2\) matrix \(Z\),
\[
\boxed{
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(Z^T)=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(Z).
}
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The fixed sector is
\[
\mathfrak f=\operatorname{span}\{I,S\},
\]
where \(I\) is the trace/diagonal component and \(S\) is the symmetric
off-diagonal component.  Both are invariant under transpose.
\end{proof}
```

---

## 5. Add leading \(Y\)-middle parity lemma

**Placement:** after the transpose lemma.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Leading \(Y\)-middle parity table]
\label{lem:leading-y-middle-parity-table}
For the homogeneous scalar-grade source table, the leading mixed-middle source
input satisfies
\[
\boxed{
Y^{[a]}_{\rm lead}=0\qquad(a\ \mathrm{odd}),
}
\]
and
\[
\boxed{
(Y^{[a]}_{\rm lead})^T=Y^{[a]}_{\rm lead}\qquad(a\ \mathrm{even}).
}
\]
In particular, for the order-\(7\) fourth-transfer targets,
\[
Y^{[1]}_1=Y^{[3]}_3=Y^{[5]}_5=0,
\]
while
\[
Y^{[0]}_0,\qquad Y^{[2]}_2,\qquad Y^{[4]}_4
\]
are symmetric.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
The corrected mixed source input is source-linear in the midpoint-centered
variables \(U_\pm\) and in the phase-gap term \(V\).  Under the homogeneous
scalar-grade convention, midpoint parity makes the leading mixed contribution
odd for odd \(a\), and the leading symmetric middle matrix vanishes in that
case.  For even \(a\), the leading term is midpoint-even and lies in the
symmetric sector.  This is the local leading-order table required here; it is
separate from, but compatible with, the homogeneous \(Y\)-slot absence used in
Gate~A.
\end{proof}
```

---

## 6. Add the general amplitude cancellation theorem

**Placement:** after the parity lemmas.

```tex
\begin{theorem}[Odd total-grade subset-complement cancellation]
\label{thm:odd-total-grade-subset-complement-cancellation}
Let
\[
J=(g_1,\ldots,g_d)
\]
be a labelled homogeneous source multiset with total grade
\[
|J|=\sum_i g_i=7.
\]
Then the leading ordinary-order amplitude contribution of the corrected-
whitening transfer vanishes in fixed sector:
\[
\boxed{
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\mathcal T_d(X^{[g_1]},\ldots,X^{[g_d]})=0.
}
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We split the transfer into the \(N_0\)-middle and \(Y\)-middle parts.

For the \(N_0\)-middle part, the leading contribution is
\[
\mathcal A_{N_0}(J)
=
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\sum_{I\subseteq J}L_I^-\,N_0[0]\,R^+_{J\setminus I}.
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:all-subset-midpoint-parity-same-point-whitening},
\[
R^+_{J\setminus I}=(-1)^{|J|-|I|}L^-_{J\setminus I}.
\]
Since \(|J|=7\), this sign is \(-(-1)^{|I|}\).  Pair the term indexed by
\(I\) with the term indexed by \(J\setminus I\).  The paired contribution is
\[
(-1)^{|I|}
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\left(
-L_I^-N_0[0]L^-_{J\setminus I}
+
L^-_{J\setminus I}N_0[0]L_I^-
\right).
\]
The three matrices \(L_I^-\), \(N_0[0]\), and \(L^-_{J\setminus I}\) are
symmetric; hence
\[
(L_I^-N_0[0]L^-_{J\setminus I})^T
=
L^-_{J\setminus I}N_0[0]L_I^-.
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:transpose-invariance-fixed-sector-projection}, the fixed-
sector projections of these two products are equal.  Their signs are opposite,
so each subset-complement pair cancels.

For the \(Y\)-middle part, fix the source label \(m\in J\) placed in the middle
slot and write \(a=g_m\), \(K=J\setminus\{m\}\).  The contribution is
\[
\sum_{I\subseteq K}
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\left(L_I^-Y_mR^+_{K\setminus I}\right).
\]
If \(a\) is odd, Lemma~\ref{lem:leading-y-middle-parity-table} gives
\(Y_m=0\) at leading order.  If \(a\) is even, then \(Y_m\) is symmetric, and
\[
|K|=7-a
\]
is odd.  The same subset-complement pairing as above applies with \(Y_m\) in
place of \(N_0[0]\).  Thus the \(Y\)-middle part also has zero fixed-sector
projection.
\end{proof}
```

---

## 7. Add the application corollary for \(d=4,5,6,7\)

**Placement:** after the amplitude theorem.

```tex
\begin{corollary}[Amplitude closure of the positive-grade Gate B transfers]
\label{cor:amplitude-closure-positive-grade-gate-b-transfers}
The amplitude part of every positive-grade Gate~B transfer of ordinary order
\(7\) vanishes in \(\mathfrak f_h\).  In particular,
\[
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\mathcal T_4
\]
vanishes on the grade multisets
\[
(1,1,1,4),\qquad (1,1,2,3),\qquad (1,2,2,2),
\]
so
\[
V_4=0.
\]
Similarly, the first lower-shadow fourth-transfer target
\[
R_{0115}
=
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\operatorname{Sym}\mathcal T_4(X^{[0]},X^{[1]},X^{[1]},X^{[5]})
\]
also vanishes.  The amplitude parts for
\[
d=5:\quad (1,1,1,1,3),\ (1,1,1,2,2),
\]
\[
d=6:\quad (1,1,1,1,1,2),
\]
and
\[
d=7:\quad (1,1,1,1,1,1,1)
\]
vanish by the same theorem.
\end{corollary}
```

---

## 8. Add the raw source derivative split

**Placement:** after the amplitude corollary.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Raw midpoint derivative of a homogeneous scalar-grade source]
\label{lem:raw-midpoint-derivative-homogeneous-source}
For
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_g r(h;t)
=
\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+2)!}(t-h)^{g+2},
\]
one has
\[
\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r
=
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r)_{\rm same}
+
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r)_{\rm lower},
\]
where
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r)_{\rm same}
=
\frac{r^{(g+3)}(h)}{(g+2)!}(t-h)^{g+2},
\]
and
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r)_{\rm lower}
=
-\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+1)!}(t-h)^{g+1}.
\]
The same-grade term has homogeneous grade \(g\), while the lower term has
homogeneous grade \(g-1\).
\end{lemma}
```

---

## 9. Add the moving source-frame telescoping theorem

**Placement:** immediately after the derivative split.

```tex
\begin{theorem}[Moving homogeneous source-frame telescoping]
\label{thm:moving-homogeneous-source-frame-telescoping}
For every interior grade \(g\ge1\),
\[
\boxed{
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g-1}r)_{\rm same}
+
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g}r)_{\rm lower}
=0.
}
\]
Consequently, after labelled symmetrization, the grade-lowering part of the
raw source-motion insertion cancels source-slot by source-slot against the
same-basis derivative of the adjacent lower grade.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
By Lemma~\ref{lem:raw-midpoint-derivative-homogeneous-source},
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g-1}r)_{\rm same}
=
\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+1)!}(t-h)^{g+1},
\]
whereas
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g}r)_{\rm lower}
=
-\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+1)!}(t-h)^{g+1}.
\]
The signs and factorials agree exactly, hence their sum is zero.

Because the cancellation is labelled-slot local, it survives the symmetrized
multilinear transfer expansion: fixing a labelled source slot \(s\), the term
coming from \((\partial_hX_s^{[g]})_{\rm lower}\) appears in the same
multilinear placement with the same remaining labelled inputs and the same
symmetrization weight as the term coming from
\((\partial_hX_s^{[g-1]})_{\rm same}\), with opposite coefficient.
\end{proof}
```

---

## 10. Add the boundary-mode lemma

**Placement:** after the telescoping theorem.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Affine boundary removal in the moving source frame]
\label{lem:affine-boundary-removal-moving-source-frame}
The \(\mathfrak D_Q\)-scaled corrected source block uses \(2\)-jet normalized
source inputs at the moving midpoint:
\[
f(h)=0,\qquad f'(h)=0.
\]
Equivalently, the source-frame projection is
\[
\Pi_{\ge2,h}f(t)=f(t)-f(h)-f'(h)(t-h).
\]
Thus constant and affine modes are not independent raw source inputs.

In particular, for
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_0 r(h;t)=\frac{r^{(2)}(h)}{2!}(t-h)^2,
\]
the lower boundary term
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_0 r)_{\rm lower}
=
-r^{(2)}(h)(t-h)
\]
is affine and is killed by \(\Pi_{\ge2,h}\).  Therefore no grade \(-1\)
source term enters
\[
[z^7][\delta^1]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
The Taylor source tower is differentiated before ordinary-order truncation.
Thus, for ordinary order \(7\), grade \(8\) is included before applying
\(\partial_h\), and truncation is applied only afterward.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
An affine perturbation \(\psi(t)=t-h\) satisfies
\[
\psi(t_\pm)=\pm\frac{z}{2Q^2},\qquad
\psi'(t_\pm)=1,\qquad
\psi''(t_\pm)=0.
\]
In the \(\mathfrak D_Q\)-scaled source variables
\[
d_\pm(z)=\frac{z^2}{Q^4}U_\pm(z),\qquad
e_\pm(z)=\frac{z}{Q^2}E_\pm(z),
\]
an affine source would force \(U_\pm\) and \(E_\pm\) to have poles in \(z\).
Hence affine modes are outside the admissible holomorphic source sector.
This is the source-frame reason the homogeneous scalar-grade tower begins at
\(g=0\), i.e. at the second derivative.
\end{proof}
```

---

## 11. Add the Gate B raw edge-divisibility corollary

**Placement:** after the boundary lemma.

```tex
\begin{corollary}[Gate B raw edge-divisibility through ordinary order \(7\)]
\label{cor:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility-order-seven}
Assume:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Gate~A aggregate closure;
\item all-subset midpoint parity for same-point inverse-square-root
coefficients;
\item symmetry of same-point inverse-square-root subset coefficients and of
\(N_0[0]\);
\item transpose-invariance of \(\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\);
\item the leading \(Y\)-middle parity table;
\item moving homogeneous source-frame telescoping;
\item affine boundary removal by \(2\)-jet source-frame normalization; and
\item differentiation of the full Taylor source tower before ordinary-order
truncation.
\end{enumerate}
Then
\[
\boxed{
[z^7][\delta^1]\,
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}=0.
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
}
\]
through ordinary order \(7\) in fixed sector.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
Gate~A removes the leading homogeneous cubic contribution.  By
Theorem~\ref{thm:odd-total-grade-subset-complement-cancellation}, all
amplitude terms of total homogeneous grade \(7\) vanish in fixed sector.  The
same-grade part of the source-motion insertion has the same midpoint parity
as the undifferentiated source and therefore vanishes by the same argument.
The grade-lowering part of the source-motion insertion cancels across adjacent
moving Taylor grades by
Theorem~\ref{thm:moving-homogeneous-source-frame-telescoping}.  The lower
affine boundary is removed by
Lemma~\ref{lem:affine-boundary-removal-moving-source-frame}; the upper
boundary is removed by differentiating the full Taylor source tower before
truncating.  Hence the entire \([z^7][\delta^1]\) fixed-sector coefficient of
the non-\((1,1)\) raw mixed package is zero.
\end{proof}
```

---

## 12. Add a guardrail remark on what was not proved by raw vanishing

**Placement:** after the Gate B corollary.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Unsymmetrized subchannels versus aggregate cancellation]
\label{rem:unsymmetrized-subchannels-versus-aggregate-cancellation}
Individual fourth-transfer placements can have nonzero fixed-sector
components.  For example, a diagonal same-point subchannel in the
\((1,1,1,4)\) class can produce a nonzero \(I\)-component.  This does not
contradict the cancellation above.  The cancellation is an aggregate statement:
it pairs each labelled subset with its complement, uses midpoint parity, and
then projects to the transpose-invariant fixed sector.  Thus no family-by-family
or placement-by-placement vanishing is asserted.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Layering of the Gate B argument]
\label{rem:layering-gate-b-argument}
The Gate~B edge-divisibility statement is raw and pre-normalization.  It must
not be replaced by the post-edge quotient statement
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C)=Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C)
\]
until the normalized package exists holomorphically.  The role of
Corollary~\ref{cor:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility-order-seven} is precisely to
supply the raw \(\delta^2\)-edge divisibility needed before forming the
normalized fixed-target quotient package.
\end{remark}
```

---

# 1. Add a new subsection defining Gate A and Gate B

**Placement:** Put this before the current UV-026 / corrected package frontier discussion, preferably before the first place the draft mentions “leading homogeneous gate”, “Gate B”, or the corrected package theorem obligations.

```tex
\subsection{Gate A and Gate B}
\label{subsec:gate-a-gate-b}

The corrected-package audit uses two separate gates.  We call them Gate~A and
Gate~B to prevent the leading homogeneous cubic audit from being confused with
the full raw edge-divisibility theorem.

\begin{definition}[Gate A: leading homogeneous cubic audit]
\label{def:gate-a-leading-homogeneous-cubic-audit}
Work under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_a r
=
\frac{r^{(a+2)}(h)}{(a+2)!}(t-h)^{a+2}.
\]
Gate~A is the audit of the leading homogeneous cubic non-\((1,1)\) source
sector at ordinary order \(7\).  In the active convention, this is the
leading \((1,1,5)\)-type cubic sector.  The Gate~A families are
\[
L_1YR_1,\qquad L_2YR_0,\qquad L_0YR_2,
\]
\[
L_2N_0R_1,\qquad L_1N_0R_2,
\]
and
\[
L_3N_0R_0,\qquad L_0N_0R_3.
\]
Gate~A asks whether these leading homogeneous non-\((1,1)\) cubic terms
produce a fixed-sector order-\(7\) obstruction.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Gate B: raw edge-divisibility]
\label{def:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility}
Gate~B is the raw edge-divisibility theorem for the full projected corrected-
whitening cross-effect.  Let
\[
C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
\mathcal W(\mathcal B_2)
-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_1,h_1))
-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_2,h_2))
+\mathcal W(\mathcal B_0),
\]
where
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}.
\]
Apply the source-bidegree projection before fixed-sector extraction,
determinant scalarization, or quotienting:
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
On the collision chart
\[
h_1=h-\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad
h_2=h+\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad
a_2=-a_1(1+\kappa\delta),
\]
Gate~B is the assertion
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
\]
through fixed-sector ordinary order \(7\).  Equivalently, the first-collision
coefficient
\[
F_1:=
[z^7][\delta^1]\,
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\]
vanishes.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Why Gate B must be raw]
\label{rem:why-gate-b-must-be-raw}
Gate~B must be proved before forming the normalized package
\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
If \(F_1\ne0\), this normalized expression has a \(\delta^{-1}\)-pole and
the post-edge package is not holomorphic.  Therefore one must not use the
post-edge quotient statement
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C)=Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C)
\]
as the first proof of edge divisibility.  Quotient compatibility is a
post-edge statement unless it is reformulated at the raw edge level.
\end{remark}
```

---

# 2. Add Gate A order exhaustion and closure

**Placement:** immediately after `subsec:gate-a-gate-b`, or after the existing `rem:first-live-non-y-cubic-gate`.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Order exhaustion for Gate A]
\label{lem:gate-a-order-exhaustion}
Under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention, every leading homogeneous cubic
non-\((1,1)\) contribution to \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\) is one of the seven
families
\[
L_1YR_1,\quad L_2YR_0,\quad L_0YR_2,\quad
L_2N_0R_1,\quad L_1N_0R_2,\quad
L_3N_0R_0,\quad L_0N_0R_3.
\]
The three \(Y\)-slot families
\[
L_1YR_1,\qquad L_2YR_0,\qquad L_0YR_2
\]
are absent at \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\).  The non-\(Y\) families reduce to the
leading placements
\[
L_2^{\{1,1\}}N_0[0]R_1^{[5]},
\qquad
L_2^{\{1,5\}}N_0[0]R_1^{[1]},
\]
and their right-side mirrors, together with the all-left/all-right placements
\[
L_3^{\{1,1,5\}}N_0[0]R_0,
\qquad
L_0N_0[0]R_3^{\{1,1,5\}}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
The \(Y\)-slot absence is the homogeneous scalar-grade order-count statement:
under this convention the relevant \(Y\)-middle entries do not reach ordinary
order \(7\).  The remaining non-\(Y\) placements are the only ones whose
Fréchet degree and homogeneous source grades can sum to \(7\) with the
baseline middle \(N_0[0]\).  This is scoped to the homogeneous scalar-grade
convention and does not apply to an ordinary matrix-output \(M^{[5]}\)
convention.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Aggregate closure of the leading \(L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2\) gate]
\label{lem:aggregate-closure-leading-l2n0r1-l1n0r2-gate}
Work under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention.  Let
\[
N:=N_0[0],
\qquad
W:=W_{-,0}=G_0^{-1/2},
\]
and let \(\Sigma_-\) denote the Sylvester inverse
\[
G_0^{1/2}Z+ZG_0^{1/2}=C.
\]
The leading same-point source inputs satisfy
\[
X_{-,1}^{[1]}=-\frac{1}{48\pi Q^2}(I-D),
\]
and
\[
X_{-,5}^{[5]}
=
-\frac{1}{92160\pi Q^{10}}(I-D)
=
\lambda X_{-,1}^{[1]},
\qquad
\lambda:=\frac{1}{1920Q^8}.
\]
Set
\[
A:=S_{-,1}^{[1]}=\Sigma_-(X_{-,1}^{[1]}),
\qquad
M:=WAW,
\]
and
\[
T_2^{1,1}:=\Sigma_-(-2AA),
\qquad
H:=2WAWAW-WT_2^{1,1}W.
\]
Then
\[
S_{-,5}^{[5]}=\lambda A,
\qquad
T_6^{1,5}=\lambda T_2^{1,1},
\qquad
H_{-,6}^{1,5}=\lambda H.
\]
Consequently the leading fixed-sector tag vectors satisfy
\[
C_{112}^{L_2N_0R_1}
=
C_{122}^{L_2N_0R_1}
=
\lambda\,\pi_{\mathfrak f}(HNM),
\]
and
\[
C_{112}^{L_1N_0R_2}
=
C_{122}^{L_1N_0R_2}
=
-\lambda\,\pi_{\mathfrak f}(MNH).
\]
Since \(H,N,M\) are symmetric and
\[
(HNM)^T=MNH,
\]
while \(\pi_{\mathfrak f}\) is transpose-invariant, one has
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(HNM)=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(MNH).
\]
Therefore
\[
C_{112}^{L_2N_0R_1}+C_{112}^{L_1N_0R_2}=0,
\]
and
\[
C_{122}^{L_2N_0R_1}+C_{122}^{L_1N_0R_2}=0.
\]
Thus the leading homogeneous non-\(Y\) \(L_2N_0R_1/L_1N_0R_2\) gate closes
at the aggregate fixed-sector level before quotienting.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Aggregate closure of the leading all-left/all-right cubic gate]
\label{lem:aggregate-closure-leading-all-left-all-right-cubic-gate}
Assume the midpoint parity convention extends to the third-Frechet coefficient
table:
\[
G^+_{n;a,b,c}=(-1)^nG^-_{n;a,b,c}.
\]
Under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention, the first all-left/all-right
source multiset is \((1,1,5)\), and it contributes at ordinary order \(7\).
Let
\[
G:=G^-_{7;1,1,5},
\qquad
W:=W_{-,0}=W_{+,0},
\qquad
N:=N_0[0].
\]
Then
\[
G^+_{7;1,1,5}=-G.
\]
The leading all-left and all-right fixed-sector contributions are
\[
C^{L_3N_0R_0}_{1,1,5}
=
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(GNW),
\]
and
\[
C^{L_0N_0R_3}_{1,1,5}
=
-\pi_{\mathfrak f}(WNG).
\]
Since \(G,N,W\) are symmetric,
\[
(GNW)^T=WNG.
\]
Because \(\pi_{\mathfrak f}\) is transpose-invariant,
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(GNW)=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(WNG).
\]
Hence
\[
C^{L_3N_0R_0}_{1,1,5}
+
C^{L_0N_0R_3}_{1,1,5}
=0
\quad\text{in }\mathfrak f.
\]
Thus the leading all-left/all-right homogeneous third-Frechet gate closes at
the aggregate fixed-sector level before quotienting.
\end{lemma}

\begin{corollary}[Gate A closure]
\label{cor:gate-a-closure}
Under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention and the stated midpoint-parity
conventions,
\[
F_1^{\mathrm{lead\ cubic}}=0.
\]
Equivalently, the leading homogeneous cubic non-\((1,1)\) sector contributes
no fixed-sector \(B_7^{\mathfrak f}\) obstruction and no first-collision
Gate~B obstruction.
\end{corollary}

\begin{remark}[Aggregate closure versus family-by-family gauge]
\label{rem:aggregate-closure-versus-family-by-family-gauge}
The preceding Gate~A closures are aggregate fixed-sector cancellations.  They
do not assert that the individual families
\[
L_2N_0R_1,\quad L_1N_0R_2,\quad L_3N_0R_0,\quad L_0N_0R_3
\]
are separately proportional to \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\).  The package theorem
requires the aggregate corrected-whitening cross-effect, not a family-by-family
gauge statement.  No determinant scalarization, no \(\Phi_K\)-scalar
invisibility, and no downstream diagonal merger is used in these closures.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Scope of Gate A]
\label{rem:scope-of-gate-a}
Gate~A is scoped to the active homogeneous scalar-grade convention.  It does
not apply to an ordinary matrix-output \(M^{[5]}\) convention, nor to a
bookkeeping system that treats lower-shadow grades \(0,2,4\) as independent
ordinary matrix-output inputs.  Such conventions require a separate
no-double-counting theorem.
\end{remark}
```

---

# 3. Add the fourth-transfer table contract

**Placement:** after the existing first/second/third Fréchet coefficient table contracts.

```tex
\subsection{Fourth-transfer coefficient table for Gate B}
\label{subsec:fourth-transfer-coefficient-table-gate-b}

The corrected-whitening map is
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}.
\]
Let a labelled source input be
\[
X_j=(A_j,Y_j,B_j),
\]
where
\[
A_j=\delta G_{-,j}^{\lin},\qquad
Y_j=\delta N_j^{\lin},\qquad
B_j=\delta G_{+,j}^{\lin}.
\]
Write
\[
(G_-^{(0)}+A)^{-1/2}
=
L_0+L_1[A]+L_2[A,A]+L_3[A,A,A]+L_4[A,A,A,A]+\cdots,
\]
and
\[
(G_+^{(0)}+B)^{-1/2}
=
R_0+R_1[B]+R_2[B,B]+R_3[B,B,B]+R_4[B,B,B,B]+\cdots.
\]
The middle block is source-linear:
\[
N=N_0+Y.
\]
For a labelled four-source set \(J=\{1,2,3,4\}\), the fourth homogeneous
corrected-whitening transfer is
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal T_4(X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4)
=
\sum_{I\subseteq J}
L^-_I\,N_0\,R^+_{J\setminus I}
+
\sum_{m\in J}
\sum_{I\subseteq J\setminus\{m\}}
L^-_I\,Y_m\,R^+_{J\setminus(I\cup\{m\})}.
}
\]
The first sum consists of
\[
L_4N_0R_0,\quad
L_3N_0R_1,\quad
L_2N_0R_2,\quad
L_1N_0R_3,\quad
L_0N_0R_4,
\]
and the second sum consists of
\[
L_3YR_0,\quad
L_2YR_1,\quad
L_1YR_2,\quad
L_0YR_3.
\]
The ordinary \(z^7\)-coefficient is
\[
\boxed{
[z^7]\mathcal T_4(J)
=
\sum_{I\subseteq J}
\sum_{\alpha+\beta+\gamma=7}
L^-_{I,\alpha}N^{(0)}_{\beta}R^+_{J\setminus I,\gamma}
+
\sum_{m\in J}
\sum_{I\subseteq J\setminus\{m\}}
\sum_{\alpha+\beta+\gamma=7}
L^-_{I,\alpha}Y_{m,\beta}R^+_{J\setminus(I\cup\{m\}),\gamma}.
}
\]
For a source multiset, \(\operatorname{Sym}\mathcal T_4\) denotes the labelled
sum divided by the automorphism factor of the multiset.
```

---

# 4. Add general parity and amplitude cancellation

**Placement:** after the fourth-transfer table.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[All-subset midpoint parity]
\label{lem:all-subset-midpoint-parity}
Let \(K\) be any labelled subset of homogeneous source inputs and define
\[
|K|:=\sum_{g\in K}g.
\]
At leading homogeneous order, the left and right same-point inverse-square-root
coefficients satisfy
\[
\boxed{
R_K^+=(-1)^{|K|}L_K^-.
}
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Symmetry of same-point subset coefficients]
\label{lem:symmetry-same-point-subset-coefficients}
For every labelled subset \(K\),
\[
(L_K^-)^T=L_K^-,
\qquad
(R_K^+)^T=R_K^+.
\]
Moreover \(N_0[0]^T=N_0[0]\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Leading \(Y\)-middle parity]
\label{lem:leading-y-middle-parity}
The leading \(Y\)-middle input satisfies
\[
Y^{[a]}_{\rm lead}=0\qquad(a\ \mathrm{odd}),
\]
and
\[
(Y^{[a]}_{\rm lead})^T=Y^{[a]}_{\rm lead}
\qquad(a\ \mathrm{even}).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Odd total-grade subset-complement cancellation]
\label{thm:odd-total-grade-subset-complement-cancellation}
Let
\[
J=(g_1,\ldots,g_d)
\]
be a labelled homogeneous source multiset with total grade
\[
|J|=\sum_i g_i=7.
\]
Then the leading ordinary-order amplitude contribution of the corrected-
whitening transfer vanishes in fixed sector:
\[
\boxed{
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\mathcal T_d(X^{[g_1]},\ldots,X^{[g_d]})=0.
}
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
For the \(N_0\)-middle part, the leading contribution is
\[
\mathcal A_{N_0}(J)
=
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\sum_{I\subseteq J}L_I^-N_0[0]R^+_{J\setminus I}.
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:all-subset-midpoint-parity},
\[
R^+_{J\setminus I}=(-1)^{|J|-|I|}L^-_{J\setminus I}.
\]
Since \(|J|=7\), this sign is \(-(-1)^{|I|}\).  Pair \(I\) with
\(J\setminus I\).  The paired contribution is
\[
(-1)^{|I|}
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\left(
-L_I^-N_0[0]L^-_{J\setminus I}
+
L^-_{J\setminus I}N_0[0]L_I^-
\right).
\]
The two matrix products are transposes, and \(\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\) is
transpose-invariant.  Hence the pair cancels.

For the \(Y\)-middle part, fix the source label \(m\in J\) in the middle and
write \(a=g_m\), \(K=J\setminus\{m\}\).  If \(a\) is odd, then
\(Y_m=0\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:leading-y-middle-parity}.  If \(a\) is even, then
\(Y_m\) is symmetric and \(|K|=7-a\) is odd, so the same subset-complement
pairing cancels the fixed-sector projection.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Amplitude closure of higher transfers]
\label{cor:amplitude-closure-higher-transfers}
The amplitude part of every Gate~B transfer of total homogeneous grade \(7\)
vanishes in \(\mathfrak f_h\).  In particular,
\[
V_4=0
\]
for the \(d=4\) positive-grade aggregate
\[
(1,1,1,4),\qquad (1,1,2,3),\qquad (1,2,2,2),
\]
and
\[
R_{0115}=0
\]
for the first lower-shadow fourth-transfer target
\[
(0,1,1,5).
\]
The same argument closes the amplitude parts for
\[
d=5:\ (1,1,1,1,3),\ (1,1,1,2,2),
\]
\[
d=6:\ (1,1,1,1,1,2),
\]
and
\[
d=7:\ (1,1,1,1,1,1,1).
\]
\end{corollary}
```

---

# 5. Add the source-motion telescoping theorem

**Placement:** after the amplitude closure.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Raw midpoint derivative of homogeneous scalar grades]
\label{lem:raw-midpoint-derivative-homogeneous-scalar-grades}
For
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_g r(h;t)
=
\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+2)!}(t-h)^{g+2},
\]
one has
\[
\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r
=
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r)_{\rm same}
+
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r)_{\rm lower},
\]
where
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r)_{\rm same}
=
\frac{r^{(g+3)}(h)}{(g+2)!}(t-h)^{g+2},
\]
and
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_g r)_{\rm lower}
=
-\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+1)!}(t-h)^{g+1}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Moving homogeneous source-frame telescoping]
\label{thm:moving-homogeneous-source-frame-telescoping}
For every interior grade \(g\ge1\),
\[
\boxed{
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g-1}r)_{\rm same}
+
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g}r)_{\rm lower}
=0.
}
\]
Consequently, after labelled symmetrization, the grade-lowering part of the
raw source-motion insertion cancels source-slot by source-slot against the
same-basis derivative of the adjacent lower grade.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
By Lemma~\ref{lem:raw-midpoint-derivative-homogeneous-scalar-grades},
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g-1}r)_{\rm same}
=
\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+1)!}(t-h)^{g+1},
\]
whereas
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{g}r)_{\rm lower}
=
-\frac{r^{(g+2)}(h)}{(g+1)!}(t-h)^{g+1}.
\]
The signs and factorials agree exactly.  The cancellation is labelled-slot
local, so it survives the symmetrized multilinear transfer expansion.
\end{proof}
```

---

# 6. Add affine-boundary removal and Gate B edge-divisibility

**Placement:** after the telescoping theorem.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Affine boundary removal in the moving source frame]
\label{lem:affine-boundary-removal-moving-source-frame}
In the \(\mathfrak D_Q\)-scaled corrected source block, admissible source
inputs are \(2\)-jet normalized at the moving midpoint:
\[
f(h)=0,\qquad f'(h)=0.
\]
Equivalently, the source-frame projection is
\[
\Pi_{\ge2,h}f(t)=f(t)-f(h)-f'(h)(t-h).
\]
Thus constant and affine modes are not independent raw source inputs.

For the homogeneous scalar-grade basis, the remaining lower boundary
\[
(\partial_h\operatorname{Gr}_{0}r)_{\rm lower}
=
-r^{(2)}(h)(t-h)
\]
is affine and is killed by \(\Pi_{\ge2,h}\).  Therefore no grade \(-1\)
source term enters
\[
[z^7][\delta^1]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
The Taylor source tower is differentiated before ordinary-order truncation;
in particular, grade \(8\) is included when proving ordinary order \(7\)
cancellation and truncation is applied only after \(\partial_h\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
An affine perturbation \(\psi(t)=t-h\) satisfies
\[
\psi(t_\pm)=\pm\frac{z}{2Q^2},\qquad
\psi'(t_\pm)=1,\qquad
\psi''(t_\pm)=0.
\]
In the \(\mathfrak D_Q\)-scaled source variables
\[
d_\pm(z)=\frac{z^2}{Q^4}U_\pm(z),
\qquad
e_\pm(z)=\frac{z}{Q^2}E_\pm(z),
\]
an affine source would force \(U_\pm\) and \(E_\pm\) to have poles in \(z\).
Hence affine modes are outside the admissible holomorphic source sector.  This
is the source-frame reason the homogeneous scalar-grade tower starts at
\(g=0\), i.e. at the second derivative.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Gate B raw edge-divisibility through ordinary order \(7\)]
\label{cor:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility-order-seven}
Assume:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Gate~A aggregate closure;
\item all-subset midpoint parity for same-point whitening coefficients;
\item symmetry of same-point whitening subset coefficients and of \(N_0[0]\);
\item transpose-invariance of \(\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\);
\item the leading \(Y\)-middle parity table;
\item moving homogeneous source-frame telescoping;
\item affine boundary removal by \(2\)-jet source-frame normalization; and
\item differentiation of the full Taylor source tower before ordinary-order
truncation.
\end{enumerate}
Then
\[
\boxed{
[z^7][\delta^1]\,
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}=0.
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
}
\]
through ordinary order \(7\) in fixed sector.
\end{corollary}
```

---

# 7. Add pre/post-trivialization notation patch

**Placement:** near `def:lower-moving-package-coefficients`.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Pre- and post-trivialization lower coefficients]
\label{rem:pre-post-trivialization-lower-coefficients}
For the quotient-line connection formula we distinguish coefficients before
and after representative-level midpoint trivialization.

Define the pre-trivialization coefficients by
\[
Z_j[M]:=
-[z^7][(h-m)^j\delta^2M]\,
a_1^{-1}
\tau_{h\to m}
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
These coefficients enter the moving-line connection formula
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega_m(Z_{7-i}[M],A_i).
\]

By contrast, \(B_j[M]\) denotes the coefficient after applying
\(\operatorname{Triv}_{h\to m}\).  Thus \(B_j[M]\) already contains the
connection contribution.  One should not insert post-trivialization coefficients
\(B_j[M]\) into the pre-trivialization connection formula, or the moving-line
correction is counted twice.
\end{remark}
```

---

# 8. Add final guardrail remarks

**Placement:** after Gate B corollary or in the UV status section.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Gate A is not Gate B]
\label{rem:gate-a-is-not-gate-b}
Gate~A is the leading homogeneous cubic audit.  Gate~B is the raw edge-
divisibility theorem for the projected corrected-whitening package.  Gate~A
closure is a necessary local input, but it is not the same statement as
edge-normalized package construction.  Corollary~\ref{cor:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility-order-seven}
records the additional transfer and source-frame mechanisms that promote the
Gate~A closure to the order-\(7\) raw edge-divisibility statement.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Unsymmetrized subchannels versus aggregate cancellation]
\label{rem:unsymmetrized-subchannels-versus-aggregate-cancellation}
Individual transfer placements can have nonzero fixed-sector components.  This
does not contradict the aggregate cancellations above.  The cancellations pair
each labelled subset with its complement, use midpoint parity, and then project
to the transpose-invariant fixed sector.  No placement-by-placement vanishing is
asserted.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Layering of the corrected package]
\label{rem:layering-of-corrected-package}
The raw edge-divisibility statement is pre-normalization.  Only after
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
\]
has been established may one form the amplitude-normalized package
\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
Post-edge quotient compatibility statements involving \(Q_{7,m}^q\) should not
be used as the first proof of edge divisibility.
\end{remark}
```

---

# 9. Replace the UV-026 / Gate status bullet

Wherever the current UV status remark lists UV-026 / Gate B as open on leading cubic terms, replace with:

```tex
\item \textbf{Gate A / UV-026 leading cubic audit.}
Under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention, the leading homogeneous cubic
non-\((1,1)\) sector is closed at the aggregate fixed-sector level.  The
\(Y\)-slot families are absent at \(B_7\), and the non-\(Y\) aggregate pairs
\[
L_2N_0R_1+L_1N_0R_2,
\qquad
L_3N_0R_0+L_0N_0R_3
\]
vanish in \(\mathfrak f\), the latter assuming third-Frechet midpoint parity.

\item \textbf{Gate B raw edge-divisibility.}
The projected raw corrected-whitening cross-effect satisfies
\[
[z^7][\delta^1]\,
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}=0
\]
through ordinary order \(7\), provided the all-subset midpoint parity,
leading \(Y\)-middle parity, moving source-frame telescoping, and affine
boundary-removal lemmas are imposed.  This supplies the raw edge divisibility
needed before amplitude normalization and before the lower package table
\(Z_j[M]\) is formed.
```

---

# 10. What this patch now captures

This patch adds the missing definitions and status split:

\[
\boxed{\text{Gate A = leading homogeneous cubic audit.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Gate B = full raw edge-divisibility / first-collision theorem.}}
\]

It also captures the full push:

\[
\boxed{\text{Gate A aggregate closure.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\mathcal T_4\text{ table contract.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{General odd total-grade amplitude cancellation.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{X_h\text{ same-grade parity and grade-lowering telescoping.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Affine lower boundary removed by \(2\)-jet source-frame normalization.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Gate B raw edge-divisibility through order \(7\).}}
\]

and the guardrails:

\[
\boxed{\text{aggregate closure is not family-by-family gauge.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{raw edge-divisibility precedes quotient compatibility.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{use \(Z_j[M]\), not post-trivialization \(B_j[M]\), in the connection sum.}}
\]

## Assistant (2026-04-25 01:43:51)

I’ll treat the latest `.tex` plus the notes as a paper-integration check. The main issue is not more algebra; it is making the narrative self-contained so a reader knows exactly what Gate A/Gate B are and why the old “open” notes are superseded.

_Thought for 51s_

Yes. The current insertion set is **directionally correct**, but it still needs a few additions to make the paper self-contained and to preserve the negative/structural findings that got us here.

The main missing items are:

1. a clean **Gate A / Gate B definition block** before any theorem uses those names;
2. a **universal first-collision formula** explaining why amplitude terms and \(X_h\)-motion terms are the two Gate B pieces;
3. a **Gate B decomposition theorem** showing how the proof pieces fit together;
4. a **negative-findings / guardrail block** preserving what failed: raw subchannel vanishing, lower-shadow absorption as a blanket reclassification, post-edge quotient compatibility used too early, etc.;
5. a **post-Gate-B package table \(Z_j[M]\)** remark, because once Gate B closes the next object becomes the pre-trivialization \(\delta^2\)-edge table;
6. a **mixed four-point sensitivity remark**: a non-gauge Gate B residual would be dangerous; a gauge residual is harmless.

The notes you attached are adaptable, but I would not include the older “Gate B remains open” claims as-is. Those notes are historically useful, but now superseded by the new parity/telescoping/affine-boundary closure. They should be converted into guardrails and “what this replaces” remarks. The older notes correctly stressed that Gate A alone did not prove Gate B and that post-edge \(Q_{7,m}^q\) cannot be used before edge divisibility; those remain important. turn160file8

Below is the full patch set I would add **on top of** the previous Gate A/Gate B patch.

---

## 1. Add a compact roadmap before the technical lemmas

**Placement:** right after `\subsection{Gate A and Gate B}` or immediately before it, if that subsection is not yet inserted.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Roadmap of the Gate A/Gate B correction]
\label{rem:roadmap-gate-a-gate-b-correction}
The corrected-package audit separates into two layers.

\emph{Gate A} is the leading homogeneous cubic audit.  It is a finite,
order-\(7\), leading-source calculation.  Under the homogeneous scalar-grade
convention, Gate~A closes at the aggregate fixed-sector level:
\[
F_1^{\mathrm{lead\ cubic}}=0.
\]

\emph{Gate B} is the raw edge-divisibility theorem.  It concerns the full
non-\((1,1)\) projected raw corrected-whitening cross-effect
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\]
on the collision chart
\[
h_1=h-\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad
h_2=h+\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad
a_2=-a_1(1+\kappa\delta).
\]
Gate~B is the assertion
\[
[z^7][\delta^1]\,
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}=0.
\]

The proof of Gate~B below uses four inputs:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Gate~A aggregate closure;
\item odd total-grade subset-complement cancellation for amplitude terms;
\item moving Taylor-frame telescoping for the source-motion terms;
\item \(2\)-jet source-frame normalization, which removes the affine lower
boundary.
\end{enumerate}
Only after Gate~B is proved may one form the normalized edge package
\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
\end{remark}
```

---

## 2. Add the universal first-collision formula

This is missing from the proposed patch, but it is the conceptual bridge between Gate A, higher-transfer amplitude cancellation, and the \(X_h\)-motion channel.

**Placement:** after the Gate B definition and before the fourth-transfer table.

```tex
\begin{lemma}[Universal first-collision formula by transfer degree]
\label{lem:universal-first-collision-formula-transfer-degree}
Let
\[
\mathcal T(X)=\sum_{d\ge1}\mathcal T_d(X)
\]
be the homogeneous transfer expansion of the corrected-whitening map.  On the
collision chart
\[
h_1=h-\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad
h_2=h+\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad
a_2=-a_1(1+\kappa\delta),
\]
the first-collision contribution of homogeneous transfer degree \(d\) has the
form
\[
\boxed{
F_{1,d}
=
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
\left[
(-1)^{d+1}d\kappa\,\mathcal T_d(X^d)
+
\frac d2(1-(-1)^d)\mathcal T_d(X^{d-1},X_h)
\right].
}
\]
Thus even \(d\) contributes only the amplitude term
\[
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\mathcal T_d(X^d),
\]
whereas odd \(d\) contributes both the amplitude term and the source-motion
term
\[
[z^7]\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\mathcal T_d(X^{d-1},X_h).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
The factor \(a_2=-a_1(1+\kappa\delta)\) produces the amplitude derivative
term proportional to \(d\kappa\).  The endpoint displacement
\(h_1=h-\delta/2\), \(h_2=h+\delta/2\) produces the midpoint-motion insertion.
The parity of \(d\) controls whether the endpoint-motion contributions cancel
or add: for even \(d\) the \(X_h\)-insertion cancels, while for odd \(d\) it
appears with coefficient \(d\).
\end{proof}
```

This lemma preserves the structural finding that the final obstruction was not “all \(p\ge4\)” but specifically the odd-\(d\) \(X_h\) channel before telescoping. 

---

## 3. Add a theorem decomposing Gate B into proof pieces

This makes the final Gate B corollary easier to believe. It also captures the full structural path.

**Placement:** immediately before `cor:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility-order-seven`.

```tex
\begin{proposition}[Decomposition of the Gate B first-collision coefficient]
\label{prop:decomposition-gate-b-first-collision}
Through ordinary order \(7\), the Gate~B first-collision coefficient decomposes
as
\[
F_1
=
F_1^{\mathrm{GateA}}
+
F_1^{\mathrm{amp}}
+
F_1^{X_h,\mathrm{same}}
+
F_1^{X_h,\mathrm{lower}}
+
F_1^{\mathrm{bdry}},
\]
where:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(F_1^{\mathrm{GateA}}\) is the leading homogeneous cubic contribution;
\item \(F_1^{\mathrm{amp}}\) is the amplitude contribution of higher transfer
terms \(\mathcal T_d(X^d)\) with total homogeneous grade \(7\);
\item \(F_1^{X_h,\mathrm{same}}\) is the grade-preserving part of the
source-motion insertion;
\item \(F_1^{X_h,\mathrm{lower}}\) is the grade-lowering part of the
source-motion insertion;
\item \(F_1^{\mathrm{bdry}}\) consists of the lower affine boundary and the
upper truncation boundary of the moving Taylor source frame.
\end{enumerate}
Under the hypotheses of Gate~A closure, all-subset midpoint parity, leading
\(Y\)-middle parity, moving source-frame telescoping, and \(2\)-jet source-frame
normalization, every term on the right vanishes.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
The decomposition follows from
Lemma~\ref{lem:universal-first-collision-formula-transfer-degree}.  Gate~A
handles the leading cubic term.  Theorem~\ref{thm:odd-total-grade-subset-complement-cancellation}
handles the amplitude part.  The grade-preserving component of \(X_h\) has the
same midpoint parity as the undifferentiated source and is handled by the same
subset-complement argument.  The grade-lowering part of \(X_h\) is handled by
Theorem~\ref{thm:moving-homogeneous-source-frame-telescoping}.  The lower
affine boundary is removed by Lemma~\ref{lem:affine-boundary-removal-moving-source-frame},
and the upper boundary is avoided by differentiating the full Taylor source
tower before truncation.
\end{proof}
```

## 4. Strengthen the affine-boundary lemma

The proposed affine-boundary lemma is good, but I would add a sentence that prevents confusion with “lower-shadow absorption.” The correct claim is not blanket reclassification of \(\operatorname{Gr}_0,\operatorname{Gr}_2,\operatorname{Gr}_4\). It is that the **affine grade \(-1\)** boundary is outside the admissible source sector, while interior lower shadows telescope. This distinction matters because earlier audits showed lower-shadow absorption as a blanket theorem is not implied by the source table. turn160file14

**Patch inside `lem:affine-boundary-removal-moving-source-frame`, after “Thus constant and affine modes are not independent raw source inputs.”**

```tex
This is not a blanket assertion that all lower-shadow grades are discarded.
The grade \(0,2,4\) source directions may appear in the moving Taylor tower.
They are handled by the interior telescoping identity when they arise from
source-motion, not by reclassifying them as baseline data.  The only boundary
mode removed here is the affine grade \(-1\) term.
```

---

## 5. Add a negative-result remark: raw vanishing of individual placements is false

The previous patch had a guardrail remark, but I would sharpen it because this was a significant negative finding.

**Placement:** after `rem:unsymmetrized-subchannels-versus-aggregate-cancellation`.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Raw placement-level vanishing is false]
\label{rem:raw-placement-level-vanishing-false}
The aggregate cancellations above must not be read as placement-level
vanishing.  A single fourth-transfer subchannel, for instance a diagonal
same-point placement in the \((1,1,1,4)\) class, can produce a nonzero
fixed-sector \(I\)-component.  The cancellation occurs only after summing the
labelled subset-complement aggregate and applying the transpose-invariant
projection \(\pi_{\mathfrak f}\).  Thus the correct theorem is aggregate
fixed-sector cancellation, not termwise support-zero or family-by-family gauge.
\end{remark}
```

---

## 6. Add a negative-result remark: post-edge quotient compatibility is too late

This is already partly in your patch, but it is important enough to state as a theorem-layer guardrail. Older notes had this as a major correction. turn161file8

**Placement:** after `rem:layering-gate-b-argument`.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Post-edge quotient compatibility is not a proof of edge divisibility]
\label{rem:post-edge-quotient-compatibility-not-edge-proof}
The formal projection identity
\[
\Pi_{1,1}\mathcal T_{d\ge3}=0
\]
does not by itself prove Gate~B.  Gate~B uses the retained complement
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw},
\]
and must be proved before the normalized quotient package exists.  Therefore
a statement such as
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C)=Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C)
\]
is a post-edge compatibility statement unless it is reformulated at the raw
edge level.  It cannot be used as the first proof that
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}=O(a_1\delta^2).
\]
\end{remark}
```

---

## 7. Add the pre-trivialization \(Z_j[M]\) table after Gate B

This is the next object after the raw edge-divisibility theorem. The notes repeatedly flagged that we must use \(Z_j\), not post-trivialization \(B_j\), to avoid double-counting. 

**Placement:** after Gate B corollary and layering remarks.

```tex
\begin{definition}[Pre-trivialization edge table after Gate B]
\label{def:pre-trivialization-edge-table-after-gate-b}
After Corollary~\ref{cor:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility-order-seven}, the
edge-normalized package is holomorphic through ordinary order \(7\).  For a
source monomial \(M\), define the pre-trivialization edge coefficient
\[
\boxed{
Z_j[M](z)
:=
-[\epsilon^j\delta^2M]\,
a_1^{-1}\,
\tau_{h\to m}\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}[z^{\le7}]
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw},
}
\]
where \(\epsilon=h-m\).  Equivalently,
\[
Z_j[M](z)
=
[\epsilon^jM]\,
\tau_{h\to m}\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}[z^{\le7}]
\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp},
\]
once the edge-normalized package \(\widehat C_{\mathcal W}^{\amp}\) has been
formed.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Pre-trivialization versus post-trivialization coefficients]
\label{rem:pre-trivialization-versus-post-trivialization-coefficients}
The coefficients \(Z_j[M]\) are pre-trivialization coefficients.  If \(B_j[M]\)
denotes coefficients after applying the representative-level midpoint
trivialization, then \(B_j[M]\) already includes the moving-line connection
contribution.  Therefore the connection sum
\[
q_{\mathrm{conn},7}[M]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{7}\omega_m(Z_{7-i}[M],A_i)
\]
must use \(Z_j[M]\), not post-trivialization \(B_j[M]\), or the connection is
counted twice.
\end{remark}
```

---

## 8. Add a mixed four-point sensitivity remark

This is worth adapting from the notes because it tells readers why Gate B matters downstream. The mixed four-point branch is sensitive only to the quotient class \([E]\); gauge residuals are harmless, non-gauge residuals are dangerous.

**Placement:** near the two-point / four-point bridge remark, or after the corrected package map.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Sensitivity of the mixed four-point branch to Gate B]
\label{rem:mixed-four-point-sensitivity-to-gate-b}
The mixed four-point branch is sensitive only to the fixed-sector quotient
class of a Gate~B residual.  Let
\[
E=eI+fS\in\mathfrak f_h.
\]
If
\[
E\in \mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h),
\]
then \(E\) is invisible in the quotient package.  On a good patch, this is
equivalently
\[
ev_5-u_5f=0,
\qquad
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5I+v_5S.
\]
If instead
\[
ev_5-u_5f\ne0,
\]
then \(E\) shifts the order-\(7\) quotient scalar:
\[
\Delta_7\longmapsto \Delta_7+(ev_5-u_5f).
\]
Consequently \(\lambda=\Delta_7/v_5\) and the downstream scalar package
\(\mathcal Z,\mathcal R,T_v\) generally change.  Thus a non-gauge Gate~B
residual would create precisely the independent fixed-sector quotient carrier
that the corrected package theorem is designed to remove.

The mixed four-point scalar-exit theorem should therefore be read after Gate~B
has removed non-\((1,1)\) quotient directions.  It is not a substitute for
Gate~B.
\end{remark}
```

This captures the structural “goodie” from the sensitivity audit: if Gate B failed non-gauge, four-point does not rescue it. 

---

## 9. Add a “superseded status” remark

This is important because the current draft may still contain older WIP statements saying Gate B is open due to \(p\ge4\) or lower shadows.

**Placement:** near the current WIP/status ledger.

```tex
\begin{remark}[Superseded Gate B status notes]
\label{rem:superseded-gate-b-status-notes}
Earlier WIP notes treated \(p\ge4\) transfer filtration and lower-shadow
absorption as open obstructions.  That was the correct status before the
homogeneous transfer audit.  The current proof replaces those open obligations
as follows:
\begin{enumerate}
\item positive-grade amplitude terms of total grade \(7\) vanish by
odd total-grade subset-complement cancellation;
\item the grade-preserving part of \(X_h\) vanishes by the same cancellation;
\item the grade-lowering part of \(X_h\) telescopes across adjacent moving
Taylor grades;
\item the sole lower boundary produced by \(\operatorname{Gr}_0\) is affine and
is removed by \(2\)-jet source-frame normalization.
\end{enumerate}
Thus the remaining role of the old notes is as guardrails: do not prove Gate~B
by post-edge quotient compatibility, do not demand family-by-family vanishing,
and do not truncate the source tower before differentiating.
\end{remark}
```

---

## 10. Small but important notation fixes

### 10.1 Use \(d\), not \(p\), for transfer degree

The notes drift between \(p\) as transfer degree and \((p,q)\) as source bidegree. I recommend adding this convention:

```tex
\begin{convention}[Transfer degree versus source bidegree]
\label{conv:transfer-degree-versus-source-bidegree}
We write \(d\) for homogeneous transfer degree in
\[
\mathcal T(X)=\sum_{d\ge1}\mathcal T_d(X),
\]
and reserve \((p,q)\) for source bidegree in the source-tagged expansion
\[
C(\tau_1,\tau_2)=\sum_{p,q}\tau_1^p\tau_2^qC_{p,q}.
\]
\end{convention}
```

### 10.2 Make \(\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\) fiber explicit

Add this if not already present:

```tex
\begin{remark}[Fixed-sector projection in the moving fiber]
\label{rem:fixed-sector-projection-moving-fiber}
When the midpoint is \(h\), the fixed-sector projection is denoted
\(\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}\).  The transpose-invariance identity
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}(Z^T)=\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}(Z)
\]
is applied in the moving midpoint fiber before midpoint trivialization to
\(m\).
\end{remark}
```

---

## 11. What I would not add

I would **not** add the older “source-bidegree quotient compatibility remains open” theorem as a live blocker anymore. It is now replaced by the raw Gate B edge-divisibility corollary. You can preserve the insight only as a layering guardrail. Older candidate lemmas like:

```tex
Q_{7,m}^q(C)=Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C)
```

should not be presented as the current route to proving Gate B. They are post-edge compatibility statements.

I would also **not** add “lower-shadow absorption” as a blanket theorem. The latest mechanism does not require blanket absorption of \(\operatorname{Gr}_0,\operatorname{Gr}_2,\operatorname{Gr}_4\); it uses moving source-frame telescoping and removes only the affine grade \(-1\) boundary by \(2\)-jet normalization.

---

## Final integration order

I would insert in this order:

1. `Gate A and Gate B` definitions.
2. `Gate A closure` lemmas and corollary.
3. `Universal first-collision formula`.
4. `Fourth-transfer table` and recurrences.
5. `All-subset midpoint parity / symmetry / Y-middle parity` lemmas.
6. `Odd total-grade subset-complement cancellation` theorem.
7. `Raw midpoint derivative` lemma.
8. `Moving source-frame telescoping` theorem.
9. `Affine boundary removal` lemma.
10. `Gate B raw edge-divisibility` corollary.
11. Guardrails: aggregate vs family, raw vs post-edge quotient, pre-trivialization \(Z_j\), mixed four-point sensitivity, superseded status notes.
12. Update the UV status ledger to say Gate A and Gate B now close through order \(7\) under the explicit source-frame/parity conventions.

This captures the whole push: structural findings, negative findings, and the positive closure mechanism.

## 1. Add the missing Gate A / Gate B definition block

**Placement:** before the current UV-026 / corrected package frontier discussion, or before the first use of “Gate A” / “Gate B”.

```tex
\subsection{Gate A and Gate B}
\label{subsec:gate-a-gate-b}

The corrected-package audit uses two separate gates.  We call them Gate~A and
Gate~B to prevent the leading homogeneous cubic audit from being confused with
the full raw edge-divisibility theorem.

\begin{definition}[Gate A: leading homogeneous cubic audit]
\label{def:gate-a-leading-homogeneous-cubic-audit}
Work under the homogeneous scalar-grade convention
\[
\operatorname{Gr}_a r
=
\frac{r^{(a+2)}(h)}{(a+2)!}(t-h)^{a+2}.
\]
Gate~A is the audit of the leading homogeneous cubic non-\((1,1)\) source
sector at ordinary order \(7\).  In the active convention, this is the
leading \((1,1,5)\)-type cubic sector.  The Gate~A families are
\[
L_1YR_1,\qquad L_2YR_0,\qquad L_0YR_2,
\]
\[
L_2N_0R_1,\qquad L_1N_0R_2,
\]
and
\[
L_3N_0R_0,\qquad L_0N_0R_3.
\]
Gate~A asks whether these leading homogeneous non-\((1,1)\) cubic terms
produce a fixed-sector order-\(7\) obstruction.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Gate B: raw edge-divisibility]
\label{def:gate-b-raw-edge-divisibility}
Gate~B is the raw edge-divisibility theorem for the full projected corrected-
whitening cross-effect.  Let
\[
C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
\mathcal W(\mathcal B_2)
-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_1,h_1))
-\mathcal W(\mathcal B_1(a_2,h_2))
+\mathcal W(\mathcal B_0),
\]
where
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}.
\]
Apply the source-bidegree projection before fixed-sector extraction,
determinant scalarization, or quotienting:
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
On the collision chart
\[
h_1=h-\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad
h_2=h+\frac{\delta}{2},\qquad
a_2=-a_1(1+\kappa\delta),
\]
Gate~B is the assertion
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
=
O(a_1\delta^2)
\]
through fixed-sector ordinary order \(7\).  Equivalently, the first-collision
coefficient
\[
F_1:=
[z^7][\delta^1]\,
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\]
vanishes.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Why Gate B must be raw]
\label{rem:why-gate-b-must-be-raw}
Gate~B must be proved before forming the normalized package
\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
If \(F_1\ne0\), this normalized expression has a \(\delta^{-1}\)-pole and
the post-edge package is not holomorphic.  Therefore one must not use the
post-edge quotient statement
\[
Q_{7,m}^q(C)=Q_{7,m}^q(\Pi_{1,1}C)
\]
as the first proof of edge divisibility.  Quotient compatibility is a
post-edge statement unless it is reformulated at the raw edge level.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Roadmap of the Gate A/Gate B correction]
\label{rem:roadmap-gate-a-gate-b-correction}
The corrected-package audit separates into two layers.

\emph{Gate A} is the leading homogeneous cubic audit.  It is a finite,
order-\(7\), leading-source calculation.  Under the homogeneous scalar-grade
convention, Gate~A closes at the aggregate fixed-sector level:
\[
F_1^{\mathrm{lead\ cubic}}=0.
\]

\emph{Gate B} is the raw edge-divisibility theorem.  It concerns the full
non-\((1,1)\) projected raw corrected-whitening cross-effect
\[
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}
\]
on the collision chart.  Gate~B is
\[
[z^7][\delta^1]\,
\pi_{\mathfrak f_h}(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}=0.
\]

The proof of Gate~B uses four inputs:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Gate~A aggregate closure;
\item odd total-grade subset-complement cancellation for amplitude terms;
\item moving Taylor-frame telescoping for source-motion terms;
\item \(2\)-jet source-frame normalization, which removes the affine lower
boundary.
\end{enumerate}
Only after Gate~B is proved may one form the normalized edge package
\[
-a_1^{-1}\delta^{-2}
(1-\Pi_{1,1})_{\rm mix}C_{\mathcal W}^{\raw}.
\]
\end{remark}
```
